Question: How do systems avoid flapping (node repeatedly going up and down)?
Answer: By using cooldown periods and multiple confirmation checks. A node must consistently pass health checks before being marked healthy again.

Question: What if the node is not down but isolated due to network partition?
Answer: This is where quorum and consensus mechanisms help. The system relies on majority agreement rather than a single node’s perspective to decide the actual state.

In real-world systems, these techniques are combined. Heartbeats detect liveness, health checks verify usability, replication lag measures performance, and quorum ensures correctness. Monitoring tools and alerting systems sit on top of these signals to notify engineers when something goes wrong.

The key takeaway is that failure detection in distributed systems is probabilistic, not absolute. You never “know” a node is down instantly—you infer it based on signals over time. Designing this correctly is crucial because incorrect detection can cause more harm than the failure itself.

Happy designing ❤️

title: A Merkle Tree is a fundamental data structure used in distri
content:
A Merkle Tree is a fundamental data structure used in distributed systems to efficiently verify large datasets without needing to transfer or compare everything. It is essentially a binary tree built using hashes, where each leaf node represents the hash of a data block, and each internal node represents the hash of its children combined. At the very top lies the Merkle Root, a single hash that uniquely represents the entire dataset.

To understand how it works, imagine you have multiple pieces of data such as A, B, C, D, E, F, G, and H. Each of these is first hashed individually. Then, pairs of hashes are combined and hashed again, forming the next level of the tree. For example, Hash(A) and Hash(B) combine to form Hash(AB), and so on. This process continues upward until a single hash is produced at the root. This root acts as a fingerprint of the entire dataset.

The key idea behind Merkle Trees is that any change in even a single data block propagates all the way up to the root. If Data F changes, its hash changes, which affects its parent hash, and ultimately changes the Merkle Root. This makes it extremely efficient to detect tampering or inconsistencies. Instead of comparing entire datasets, systems can simply compare root hashes. If the roots match, the data is identical; if not, there is a difference somewhere.

One of the biggest advantages of Merkle Trees is efficient verification. Suppose you want to verify whether a specific piece of data, say Data C, is part of the dataset. You do not need all other data blocks. You only need a small subset of hashes along the path from that leaf to the root. This is known as a Merkle Proof. Using these hashes, you can recompute the path and check if it matches the known root. This reduces the verification complexity from linear to logarithmic, which is crucial when dealing with massive datasets.

This structure becomes extremely powerful in distributed systems where nodes do not fully trust each other. Instead of sending entire data, nodes exchange Merkle Roots. If the roots differ, they recursively compare subtrees to identify exactly where the mismatch occurs. This minimizes network usage and speeds up synchronization.

Merkle Trees are widely used in systems where data integrity and efficiency are critical. In blockchains, each block contains a Merkle Root of all transactions, allowing quick verification without storing every transaction. In distributed databases and file systems, they help detect differences between replicas and synchronize only the changed parts. Version control systems also use similar concepts to track changes efficiently.

Another important aspect is partial recomputation. When data changes, the system does not need to recompute hashes for the entire dataset. Only the hashes along the path from the modified leaf to the root are recalculated. This makes updates efficient even for very large datasets.

The deeper insight is that Merkle Trees transform a large verification problem into a structured, hierarchical process. Instead of treating data as a flat collection, they organize it in a way that allows quick detection, efficient proof generation, and minimal data transfer. This combination of integrity, efficiency, and scalability is why Merkle Trees are a core building block in modern distributed systems.

In simple terms, a Merkle Tree allows you to verify massive data using very small pieces of information, making trust scalable in distributed environments.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Clustering and Replication
content:
It started like any normal day for thousands of companies running their applications on the cloud. E-commerce platforms, banking systems, delivery apps, internal tools everything quietly depended on one invisible layer: cloud infrastructure. 

But on March 1, 2026, something unprecedented happened. Iranian drone and missile strikes targeted multiple data centers in the Middle East, including facilities used by Amazon’s cloud infrastructure. Within minutes, entire availability zones went dark. Services stopped. APIs failed. Systems that millions relied on simply vanished.

This was a wake up call for the entire tech world. The “cloud” was never abstract. It was always physical servers, cables, power grids and those could be destroyed.

Now think like a system designer. If your entire application is running in one region, one data center, or even one cloud provider, what happens when that region goes down? The answer is simple: your system is gone. Not slow, not degraded - completely unavailable.

This is where the real need for replication and distributed design comes into the picture.

Let’s break this down from a system design perspective.

A single region dependency is a critical failure point. Many systems are initially built in a single region because it is simpler and cheaper. But this creates a single point of failure. In the incident above, entire regions were impacted, which means all services hosted there went offline together. The lesson is clear: region-level failures are real.

Replication is not optional at scale - it is mandatory. Data must exist in multiple geographically separated regions. This is called geo-replication. If one region is destroyed or unavailable, traffic can be shifted to another region where the same data and services are already running.

Now comes an important question.

Is simple backup enough?

No. Backups help you recover data, but they do not provide availability. If your system goes down and you restore from backup, users still experience downtime. What you need is active replication, not just passive storage.

Multi-region architecture is the real solution. Instead of having one primary system, you deploy your application in multiple regions. There are different strategies here:

Active-passive: One region serves traffic, another stays on standby. If the primary fails, traffic is switched.
Active-active: Multiple regions serve traffic simultaneously. If one fails, others continue seamlessly.

Active-active is more complex but provides near-zero downtime.

Data consistency becomes a challenge. When you replicate data across regions, you must decide between strong consistency and eventual consistency. Strong consistency ensures all regions see the same data instantly but increases latency. Eventual consistency allows slight delays but improves performance and availability.

But then a question strikes us, Why not always use strong consistency?

Because cross-region communication is slow and unreliable. In large-scale systems, availability is often prioritized over strict consistency.

Traffic routing plays a crucial role. You need intelligent routing using DNS or load balancers that can detect failures and redirect users to healthy regions. If one region is down, users should automatically connect to another without even noticing.

Disaster recovery planning is essential. This includes:

Failover strategies
Regular backups
Chaos testing (simulating failures)
Monitoring and alerting systems

The companies affected in such incidents often scramble to migrate workloads to other regions after failure, but a well-designed system should already be prepared for that.

Data durability vs availability. Replication ensures durability (data is not lost), but architecture ensures availability (system is accessible). Both are equally important. Losing data is bad, but being unavailable is also a failure from a user perspective.

In earlier times, wars targeted oil, bridges, and power grids. Today, data centers are becoming strategic targets because they power economies, AI systems, and communication networks.

This changes how we design systems. We are no longer just designing for bugs or traffic spikes, we are designing for region failures, infrastructure destruction, and geopolitical risks.

A resilient system is not the one that never fails. It is the one that continues to operate even when entire parts of it are destroyed.

That is the true goal of replication and distributed system design.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
The Domain Name System (DNS) is one of the most fundamental building blocks of the internet, yet it works silently behind the scenes. At a high level, DNS acts like a phonebook for the internet. Humans prefer to use easy-to-remember domain names like google.com, but computers communicate using IP addresses like 142.250.183.14. DNS is the system that translates these human-friendly names into machine-understandable IP addresses.

When you type a URL into your browser, your system does not immediately know the IP address of that domain. It needs to “resolve” it. This resolution process involves multiple layers of servers working together in a hierarchical manner. First, the request may check a local cache. If not found, it goes to a DNS resolver, which then queries root servers, top-level domain (TLD) servers (like .com), and finally authoritative name servers that hold the actual mapping of domain to IP. Once the IP address is found, it is returned back through the chain, and your browser can then make the actual request to the server.

Now, this is where the DNS resolver becomes the most critical component in the entire flow.

A DNS resolver is the part of the system responsible for doing the hard work of finding the correct IP address for a domain. It acts as an intermediary between your device and the global DNS infrastructure. Instead of your system querying multiple servers directly, the resolver handles all communication, recursion, caching, and optimization.

The resolver starts by checking its cache. If it has already resolved the domain recently, it immediately returns the stored IP address, making the process extremely fast. If not, it begins a recursive query process. It contacts a root server, which tells it where to find the TLD server. The TLD server then points to the authoritative server, and finally, the resolver gets the exact IP address. This entire process usually happens in milliseconds.

One of the most important aspects of a DNS resolver is caching. Once a domain is resolved, the result is stored for a certain duration known as TTL (Time To Live). This reduces repeated lookups, decreases latency, and significantly lowers the load on DNS infrastructure. Without caching, every request would require traversing the entire DNS hierarchy, making the internet slower and less efficient.

From a system design perspective, DNS resolvers play a huge role in performance and reliability. Large-scale systems often rely on distributed resolvers placed geographically closer to users. This reduces lookup time and improves user experience. Public DNS resolvers like those provided by cloud companies are optimized for speed, availability, and security.

Another critical aspect is fault tolerance. If one resolver fails, systems can switch to another. Similarly, DNS infrastructure itself is highly replicated across the globe to ensure that domain resolution never becomes a single point of failure.

There are also different types of resolvers. A stub resolver is a lightweight component present on your device that forwards queries to a recursive resolver. A recursive resolver does the full resolution process. Authoritative servers, on the other hand, do not resolve queries further—they simply provide the final answer for domains they manage.

Understanding this interaction is important.

Why doesn’t your browser directly query root servers?
Because that would be inefficient and complex. The resolver abstracts this complexity and optimizes the process.

Why is DNS resolution usually fast even though it involves multiple steps?
Because of caching at multiple layers—browser cache, OS cache, resolver cache, and even ISP-level caching.

What happens if a resolver is slow or down?
Your entire internet experience feels slow or broken, even if the actual websites are working fine.

In modern system design, DNS resolvers are also used for intelligent routing. Techniques like GeoDNS route users to the nearest server based on their location. This helps in load balancing, latency reduction, and high availability. DNS is no longer just a lookup system—it is a critical traffic management layer.

The deeper insight is that DNS, and specifically the resolver, transforms a complex, distributed lookup problem into a fast and seamless experience. It hides multiple network hops, ensures efficiency through caching, and provides resilience through distributed design.

In simple terms, the DNS resolver is the invisible engine that makes the internet feel instant and reliable.

Happy designing ❤️

title: You upload photos to a cloud app today, and even after years
content:
You upload photos to a cloud app today, and even after years, you still expect them to be there. But here’s the real question: should all that data live on fast, expensive storage forever? 

What about logs from last year, old backups, or rarely accessed documents? Keeping everything on high performance systems is not just inefficient, it becomes extremely expensive at scale. This is where the idea of cold storage comes into system design.

Cold storage refers to storing data that is rarely accessed but must be retained for long periods. Instead of keeping it on fast, low latency systems like databases or in-memory caches, we move it to cheaper, slower storage designed for durability rather than speed. The goal is simple: optimize cost without losing data.

Let’s understand the problem first. In most applications, data access follows a pattern. Recent data is accessed frequently, while older data is rarely touched. For example, in a payment system, transactions from the last few days are queried often, but transactions from two years ago are almost never accessed unless for audits. If you store both types of data in the same high performance database, you are paying a huge cost for something you don’t really need.

So how do systems handle this?

They introduce data tiering. Data is classified based on how frequently it is accessed. Hot data stays in fast systems like RAM or SSD backed databases. Warm data may be stored in slightly slower systems. Cold data is pushed to long-term storage systems such as object storage or archival services.

Now think about the flow.

User request → Application → Fast database (hot data check)
If not found → Query archival system (cold storage) → Retrieve → Respond

This layered approach ensures that most requests are fast while still allowing access to older data when needed.

But this introduces an important question.

If cold storage is slow, won’t user experience suffer?

The answer depends on how you design access patterns. Cold storage is not meant for real time queries. If a request involves cold data, the system may take seconds instead of milliseconds. In many cases, systems handle this asynchronously. For example, when you request old data, the system may notify you later once it is retrieved. Think about how email services allow you to restore archived emails or how cloud storage retrieves archived files after some delay.

Another key aspect is cost optimization. Storage systems like object storage are significantly cheaper than high performance databases. Cold storage solutions can reduce storage costs by orders of magnitude. That is why companies move logs, backups, analytics data, and historical records into cold storage.

Now let’s talk about durability.

Cold storage systems are designed to be extremely durable. Data is often replicated across multiple locations or stored with redundancy mechanisms like erasure coding. Even if hardware fails, the data remains safe. The tradeoff is higher latency for retrieval.

Let’s explore how data moves into cold storage.

This process is called data lifecycle management. Based on rules (like move data older than 90 days), the system automatically transfers data from hot storage to cold storage. This can be done using background jobs or storage policies.

Now think deeper.

What happens if you suddenly need cold data frequently again?

Systems may “rehydrate” data, moving it back to warm or hot storage. This is common in analytics workloads where old data becomes relevant again.

Let’s challenge your understanding.

Why not store everything in cold storage to save cost?
Because latency would become unacceptable for user facing operations.

Why not keep everything in hot storage?
Because cost would become unsustainable as data grows.

So system design is about balancing cost, performance, and access patterns.

Real-world examples make this clearer.

Streaming platforms store recent content metadata in fast databases, but older logs and viewing history are archived. Banking systems store recent transactions in primary databases but move older records to archival systems for compliance. Cloud providers offer different storage tiers specifically for this purpose.

From a system design perspective, cold storage is not just about storage it is about designing intelligent data flows. You decide what data needs speed and what data needs durability. You design systems that automatically move data across tiers. You ensure that even rarely accessed data is still available when needed, without affecting system performance.

The deeper insight is not all data is equal. Treating all data the same leads to inefficiency. Good system design recognizes access patterns and optimizes accordingly.

Cold storage is how large-scale systems stay both cost efficient and reliable over time.

Happy designing ❤️

title: When traffic drops, most systems still keep running at full
content:
When traffic drops, most systems still keep running at full capacity, and that becomes a silent problem. You end up paying for machines that are doing almost nothing. This is exactly where downscaling in system design becomes important. It is not just an optimization technique, but a core principle for building cost-efficient and scalable systems.

In a typical system, infrastructure is designed to handle peak traffic. During high load, multiple servers are active to process requests efficiently. However, traffic is never constant. There are always periods of low demand. If the same number of servers continue running during these periods, the system has excess capacity that is not being utilized. Downscaling addresses this by reducing the number of active resources when demand decreases.

From a system design perspective, downscaling means dynamically adjusting infrastructure based on real-time load. When system metrics such as CPU usage, memory consumption, or request rate fall below a certain threshold for a sustained period, the system identifies underutilized instances and safely terminates them. This results in a right-sized architecture that aligns resources with actual demand.

A natural question arises: why not keep all servers running all the time? The answer lies in cost and efficiency. Infrastructure cost scales with the number of active resources, but user traffic does not remain constant. Without downscaling, you are effectively paying peak costs even during off-peak hours. This leads to poor resource utilization and unnecessary expenses.

Another important question is whether downscaling is risky. The answer is yes, if done incorrectly. Removing resources too aggressively can lead to performance degradation if traffic suddenly increases. To handle this, systems use carefully defined thresholds, cooldown periods, and sometimes predictive scaling based on historical patterns. The goal is to ensure stability while still optimizing costs.

It is also important to understand how systems decide when to downscale. They continuously monitor metrics such as average CPU usage, request latency, queue lengths, and throughput. If these metrics remain below predefined limits for a certain duration, the system gradually reduces the number of instances. This gradual approach helps avoid sudden disruptions.

Another key aspect is graceful shutdown. When a server is selected for removal, it does not terminate immediately. Instead, it stops accepting new requests and completes any ongoing operations before shutting down. This ensures that user experience is not affected during the scaling process.

You might wonder why not handle this manually. The reason is that traffic patterns are unpredictable and can change rapidly. Automated scaling systems react in real time, making them far more reliable than manual intervention. Automation ensures that the system remains efficient without constant human oversight.

An interesting observation is that downscaling is often more complex than upscaling. Adding servers is relatively straightforward, but removing them requires careful handling to maintain system stability. This is why well-designed systems treat downscaling as a controlled and gradual process.

In real-world applications, downscaling plays a crucial role. Streaming platforms reduce infrastructure during late-night hours, e-commerce platforms scale down after sales events, and cloud-native applications continuously adjust resources based on demand patterns. These systems are designed to expand when required and shrink when possible.

The deeper takeaway is that scalability is not just about handling growth. It is about efficiently handling both increase and decrease in demand. A truly scalable system is one that maintains performance during peaks and minimizes waste during lows. That balance is what makes system design effective in real-world scenarios.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Clustering and Replication
content:
In modern distributed systems, a single user request rarely gets handled by just one service. Instead, it flows through multiple services, each responsible for a specific task. This is where orchestration comes into play. Orchestration is the centralized coordination of multiple services to complete a workflow in a controlled and predictable manner.

In the architecture shown, the user (via a web browser) sends a request to an orchestrator. This orchestrator acts like the brain of the system. Instead of the client directly calling multiple services, the orchestrator decides which services need to be invoked, in what order, and how the data flows between them. It then communicates with Service A, Service B, and Service C, each of which may have its own database and responsibility.

A natural question is: why introduce an orchestrator instead of letting services communicate directly? The answer lies in control and simplicity. Without orchestration, each service would need to know about other services, leading to tight coupling and complex interdependencies. With an orchestrator, the logic is centralized. Services remain independent and focused only on their specific tasks, while the orchestrator handles the workflow.

Another important aspect is sequencing. Not all operations can happen in parallel. Some tasks depend on the output of others. The orchestrator ensures the correct order of execution. For example, Service A might validate data, Service B might process it, and Service C might store or notify. The orchestrator ensures this sequence is followed reliably.

You might wonder if this creates a single point of failure. That is a valid concern. If the orchestrator goes down, the entire workflow can be impacted. To solve this, production systems deploy orchestrators in a highly available manner, often with replication and failover strategies. This ensures reliability while still benefiting from centralized control.

Another key advantage is observability. Since all requests pass through the orchestrator, it becomes easier to track workflows, debug failures, and monitor performance. If something breaks, you know exactly where in the flow the issue occurred. This is extremely useful in complex systems where tracing distributed calls can be challenging.

A common follow-up question is how this differs from choreography. In choreography, there is no central controller. Services react to events and communicate among themselves. In orchestration, everything is directed by a central entity. Both approaches have their use cases, but orchestration is preferred when workflows are complex and need strict control.

In real-world systems, orchestration is widely used in scenarios like order processing, payment workflows, booking systems, and multi-step data pipelines. Tools like workflow engines and orchestration frameworks help manage retries, failures, and state transitions automatically.

The deeper insight is that orchestration simplifies complexity at the cost of introducing a central coordinator. When designed correctly, it provides clarity, control, and reliability in distributed systems, making it easier to scale and maintain complex workflows.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Kafka Fundamentals and Event Streaming
content:
When you open a live match on Hotstar, it feels like a simple play button, but behind the scenes there is a massive distributed system working to handle millions, sometimes crores of users simultaneously. The diagram shows a simplified but powerful pipeline of how such a system processes, distributes, and scales live streaming traffic.

The flow starts from client apps, which can be Android, iOS, or web browsers. These clients send HTTP POST requests to backend services whenever a user joins a stream, interacts, or requests data. This request is handled by a Golang service, which acts as a lightweight, high-performance entry point. Golang is often chosen here because of its efficiency in handling concurrent requests at scale.

Once the request is received, it is not processed synchronously for every user. Instead, the system pushes events into Kafka, which acts as the backbone of real-time data streaming. This is a critical design decision. Rather than directly handling everything in real time per request, the system decouples ingestion from processing. Kafka ensures durability, scalability, and fault tolerance, meaning even if downstream services fail, data is not lost.

From Kafka, the data flows into multiple paths. One path goes to Amazon S3 via Kafka Connect for archival purposes. This is important because streaming platforms store massive amounts of historical data for analytics, replay, recommendations, and compliance. Another path sends data to Amazon EMR, where large-scale batch processing happens. This includes analytics like viewer behavior, peak load times, engagement metrics, and recommendation model training.

At the same time, there is a real-time processing pipeline. A Python consumer reads events from Kafka and publishes processed information to a Pub/Sub system. This Pub/Sub layer is responsible for pushing updates to online clients, such as live score updates, chat messages, or viewer counts. This ensures that users receive near real-time updates without overwhelming the core system.

Now think about scale. If 10 million users join within a few seconds, the system does not collapse because Kafka buffers the load, consumers scale horizontally, and services remain decoupled. Each component can scale independently. If ingestion increases, add more Kafka partitions. If processing lags, add more consumers. If delivery becomes a bottleneck, scale Pub/Sub nodes.

Interactive question: why not directly send updates from backend to all users instead of using Kafka and Pub/Sub? Because direct communication creates tight coupling and cannot handle sudden spikes. Kafka acts as a shock absorber, while Pub/Sub ensures efficient fan-out to millions of clients.

Another important aspect is fault tolerance. If the Python consumer crashes, Kafka still retains the events. When the consumer restarts, it resumes from where it left off. Similarly, if EMR jobs fail, data can be reprocessed from S3. This design ensures no data loss and high reliability.

Real-world insight: systems like Hotstar during IPL or World Cup matches handle traffic comparable to entire countries logging in at once. Without such event-driven, distributed architecture, even a small spike would bring the system down.

This architecture teaches a key system design principle: separate ingestion, processing, storage, and delivery. Each layer should scale independently and fail independently without bringing down the entire system.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
Caching looks simple on the surface: store frequently accessed data closer to the application and reduce latency. But the real challenge is not caching itself, it is deciding what to remove when the cache becomes full. This is where cache eviction policies turn into one of the most difficult decisions in system design.

At its core, a cache is always limited in size. Memory is expensive, and you cannot store everything. So the moment your cache reaches capacity, the system must decide which data to evict to make space for new entries. This decision directly impacts performance, cost, and user experience. If you evict the wrong data, you increase cache misses, which means more database calls, higher latency, and increased load on backend systems.

The difficulty arises because “importance” of data is not absolute. It is contextual. Some data is accessed frequently, some data was accessed recently, and some data might be expensive to recompute. Different applications prioritize these factors differently. This is why multiple eviction strategies exist, such as Least Recently Used (LRU), Least Frequently Used (LFU), First In First Out (FIFO), and even hybrid approaches. Each works well in certain scenarios but fails in others.

For example, LRU assumes that recently accessed data will be accessed again soon. This works well for temporal locality, like user sessions. But consider a case where a large dataset is scanned once. LRU will fill the cache with this one-time data and evict more useful entries, causing performance degradation. Similarly, LFU favors frequently accessed data, but it can hold onto stale data that was once popular but is no longer needed.

Another layer of complexity comes from real-world traffic patterns. Workloads are not static. A system may experience sudden spikes, seasonal trends, or shifting user behavior. A cache policy that works perfectly under normal load might fail during peak traffic. For instance, during a flash sale, product data access patterns change completely, and your eviction strategy must adapt accordingly.

Interactive question: why not just increase cache size and avoid eviction complexity?
Answer: because memory is not infinite and scaling cache blindly increases cost. Also, even large caches eventually face eviction decisions under high throughput systems.

Consistency also plays a role. If cached data becomes stale and is not evicted or updated properly, users may see outdated information. This is critical in systems like banking or inventory management. So eviction is not just about freeing space, it is also about maintaining correctness.

Another challenge is distributed caching. In systems like Redis clusters, data is spread across multiple nodes. Now eviction decisions are happening independently on different nodes, which can lead to uneven cache efficiency. Some nodes may evict critical data while others still hold less useful entries. Coordinating eviction across distributed systems adds another layer of complexity.

Real-world systems often use hybrid approaches. For example, combining LRU with TTL (time-to-live) ensures that even frequently accessed data eventually expires. Some systems use adaptive policies that monitor access patterns and dynamically adjust eviction strategies. Others incorporate machine learning models to predict which data should stay in cache.

Think about platforms like Netflix or Amazon. They cache recommendations, product details, and user sessions. Evicting the wrong data can lead to slower page loads or irrelevant recommendations, directly impacting user engagement and revenue.

So the real takeaway is this: cache eviction is not just a technical decision, it is a business decision. It affects latency, infrastructure cost, scalability, and user experience all at once. There is no one-size-fits-all solution, and the best systems continuously evolve their eviction strategies based on real-world usage.

Happy designing ❤️

title: A load balancer is often treated as the single entry point t
content:
A load balancer is often treated as the single entry point to a system, which makes it one of the most critical components in the entire architecture. But that also raises a dangerous question: what happens if the load balancer itself goes down? If you rely on a single load balancer, you have essentially introduced a single point of failure, and when it fails, your entire system becomes unreachable even if all backend services are perfectly healthy.

The first principle in system design here is simple: never trust a single instance for a critical component. Instead of one load balancer, production systems always deploy multiple load balancers. These can be active-active, where multiple load balancers handle traffic simultaneously, or active-passive, where one is primary and another takes over on failure. This redundancy ensures that if one instance crashes, traffic is automatically routed to another without impacting users.

Now the next question is, how does traffic know which load balancer to hit? This is where DNS comes into play. Instead of pointing your domain to a single IP, you configure DNS with multiple IP addresses (multiple load balancers). DNS can return different IPs in a round-robin fashion or based on health checks. If one load balancer becomes unhealthy, it is removed from DNS responses, and new traffic automatically shifts to healthy ones.

Is DNS failover instant?
Answer: not always. DNS responses are cached, so there can be delays depending on TTL (time to live). This is why DNS alone is not enough for fast failover in high-availability systems.

To handle this, systems introduce an additional layer using anycast IPs or global traffic managers. In anycast, the same IP address is advertised from multiple locations, and network routing ensures that traffic goes to the nearest or healthiest load balancer. If one region fails, routing automatically shifts traffic to another region without requiring DNS changes.

Another layer of protection is health checks. Load balancers continuously monitor themselves and backend services. If a load balancer instance becomes unhealthy, orchestration systems like Kubernetes or cloud platforms automatically replace it. This is often combined with auto scaling groups, which ensure that a minimum number of load balancers are always running.

At a higher level, multi-region architecture plays a key role. Instead of having all load balancers in one region, systems distribute them across multiple geographic regions. If an entire data center goes down, traffic is rerouted to another region. This is how large-scale systems like Google, Amazon, and Netflix achieve high availability.

Then you might ask, why not just put a backup load balancer manually and switch when needed?
Answer: manual intervention is too slow and unreliable for modern systems. Failover must be automatic, fast, and transparent to users.

There is also the concept of cascading load balancing. You can have a global load balancer (like DNS or a traffic manager) that routes users to regional load balancers, which then distribute traffic to application servers. This layered approach reduces the risk of total failure and improves scalability.

Real-world insight: during major outages, it is rarely the backend services that fail first. Often, it is the entry layer like load balancers or DNS misconfigurations that bring systems down. That is why companies invest heavily in redundancy, failover strategies, and continuous health monitoring at the entry point.

The key takeaway is this: a load balancer should never be a single point of failure. It must be replicated, monitored, and distributed across regions. High availability is not about preventing failures, it is about designing systems that continue to function even when failures inevitably happen.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
If an API Gateway is your single entry point and it goes down, your entire system can appear offline even when all downstream services are perfectly healthy. This is why treating the gateway as just a routing layer is a mistake; in reality, it becomes one of the most critical components in your architecture. The moment it fails, authentication, rate limiting, routing, logging, and even basic request forwarding stop working, effectively cutting users off from your platform.

Now think deeper. Why is API Gateway failure more dangerous than a single microservice failing? Because microservices are usually isolated, but the gateway is a centralized control point. It sits in front of everything. So even a small misconfiguration, deployment bug, or scaling issue can cause a full system outage. This is why production systems never rely on a single gateway instance.

So how do real systems handle this?
The first layer of protection is redundancy. Instead of one API Gateway, you deploy multiple instances behind a load balancer. If one instance crashes, traffic is automatically routed to healthy ones. But here is the catch: what if the load balancer itself fails? That is why systems often use DNS-based failover or multiple regional load balancers to avoid a single point of failure at the entry layer.

Next comes geographic distribution. Large-scale systems deploy API Gateways across multiple regions. Traffic is routed using geo-DNS or Anycast routing, so even if an entire region goes down, users are automatically redirected to the nearest healthy region. This is how systems achieve high availability across continents.

Another important concept is graceful degradation. If the API Gateway is partially failing or overloaded, instead of completely rejecting traffic, it can fall back to limited functionality. For example, serving cached responses, disabling non-critical APIs, or routing only high-priority requests. This ensures the system does not completely collapse under stress.

Caching also plays a surprisingly powerful role here. Many API Gateways cache frequent responses. If downstream services become unreachable, cached responses can still be served temporarily, buying time for recovery. This is especially useful for read-heavy systems like content platforms.

Now consider rate limiting and circuit breaking at the gateway level. If downstream services are failing, the gateway can stop forwarding excessive traffic to prevent cascading failures. This isolates problems and protects the rest of the system.

Let’s turn this into an interview-style thinking question.

Question: If your API Gateway crashes, will your system always go down?
Answer: Not necessarily. If you design it properly with multiple gateway instances, load balancing, regional failover, and fallback mechanisms, users might not even notice the failure. The system continues to operate because there is no single point of failure.

Question: Why not bypass the API Gateway and let clients directly call services?
Answer: That removes a central control layer for authentication, monitoring, rate limiting, and routing. It increases complexity on the client side and weakens system security. Instead of removing the gateway, we make it highly available and fault tolerant.

Real-world systems like Netflix, Amazon, and Google treat API Gateways as distributed systems themselves. They are scaled horizontally, replicated across regions, monitored aggressively, and designed to fail gracefully.

The key takeaway is simple: the API Gateway is not just a router, it is a critical infrastructure component. If it goes down and you did not design for failure, your entire system goes down with it. If you design it correctly, failure becomes just another recoverable event.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
At first glance, forward proxy and reverse proxy look very similar. Both sit between a client and a server, both forward requests, and both can add control, security, and performance. But the key difference lies in who they represent and where they sit in the architecture.

Let’s start with the forward proxy, shown on the left side of the diagram. Here, clients inside a private network do not directly access the internet. Instead, all their requests go through a forward proxy. This proxy acts on behalf of the client. When a user tries to access a website, the request first hits the forward proxy, which then forwards it to the external servers on the internet. The response comes back to the proxy and then to the client. From the server’s perspective, it sees the proxy as the requester, not the actual client.

Why do we need this? Forward proxies are mainly used for control and privacy. Organizations use them to restrict access to certain websites, log user activity, cache frequently accessed content, or even hide client identity. For example, in a corporate network, employees cannot directly access the internet; all traffic goes through a forward proxy for monitoring and filtering.

Now shift your focus to the right side of the diagram, where the reverse proxy is shown. Here, the situation is flipped. Clients on the internet send requests, but they never directly reach the backend servers. Instead, they hit a reverse proxy first. This proxy represents the servers, not the clients. It receives incoming requests and forwards them to one of the backend services inside a private network.

This is where reverse proxies become extremely powerful in system design. They are used for load balancing, routing, security, caching, and SSL termination. For example, when millions of users access a website, the reverse proxy distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers, ensuring no single server gets overwhelmed. It can also hide internal architecture, so clients never know how many servers exist behind the scenes.

A simple question, why not let clients directly talk to backend servers instead of using a reverse proxy?
Answer: because it exposes internal systems, reduces security, and removes the ability to centrally manage traffic. A reverse proxy acts as a controlled entry point, adding layers like authentication, rate limiting, and monitoring.

Another important difference is visibility. In a forward proxy setup, the client knows it is using a proxy. In a reverse proxy setup, the client usually has no idea. It simply thinks it is communicating with a single server, while in reality, the reverse proxy is handling routing behind the scenes.

Let’s connect this to real-world systems. Tools like Squid are commonly used as forward proxies in enterprise networks. On the other hand, Nginx, HAProxy, and cloud load balancers act as reverse proxies in large-scale systems like Netflix, Amazon, and Google.

So the simplest way to remember this is: a forward proxy sits in front of clients and represents them, while a reverse proxy sits in front of servers and represents them. One controls outgoing traffic, the other controls incoming traffic.

This small architectural distinction has massive implications in scalability, security, and system design.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
At a high level, firewall, VPN, and proxy all sit in the path of network communication, but they solve very different problems. If you look at the diagram, each one is placed at a different layer of control, and understanding that placement is the key to not confusing them in system design.

Start with the firewall. Think of it as the gatekeeper of a network. In the diagram, it sits between the internet and internal users, inspecting incoming and outgoing traffic. Its job is simple but critical: allow or block traffic based on rules. These rules can be based on IP addresses, ports, protocols, or even deeper packet inspection. If a malicious request tries to enter, the firewall drops it before it reaches internal systems. In large systems, firewalls are the first line of defense, protecting databases, services, and internal networks from unauthorized access.

Now move to VPN. In the diagram, you see a user device connecting to a VPN client, which then communicates with a VPN server through the internet. The key idea here is encryption and secure tunneling. A VPN creates a private, encrypted connection over a public network. This means even if someone intercepts the traffic, they cannot read it. VPNs are widely used for secure remote access, for example when employees connect to a company’s internal systems from outside. Unlike firewalls, which block or allow traffic, VPNs focus on securing the communication channel itself.

Lets answer this question, if VPN encrypts traffic, does it also protect systems like a firewall?
Answer: not really. VPN secures the data in transit, but it does not decide what should or should not be allowed. That responsibility still belongs to firewalls and access control systems.

Now look at proxy servers in the diagram. Clients send requests to the proxy, which then forwards them to the internet or to internal servers. A proxy acts as an intermediary. It can hide the client’s identity, cache responses, filter requests, and even enforce policies. There are two types you should remember: forward proxy (used by clients to access the internet) and reverse proxy (used by servers to handle incoming traffic). In system design, reverse proxies are extremely common because they help with load balancing, security, and request routing.

Another question: if a proxy can filter requests, is it the same as a firewall?
Answer: no. A proxy operates at the application level and understands request content (like HTTP), while a firewall often works at network or transport layers, focusing on packets and connections. They complement each other, not replace each other.

Now compare them together. A firewall controls what traffic is allowed. A VPN secures how traffic travels. A proxy controls how requests are routed and can add caching or anonymity. In a real-world system, all three are often used together. For example, a company might use a firewall to block malicious traffic, a VPN to allow secure employee access, and a reverse proxy to distribute incoming user requests across servers.

Real-world insight: large platforms like Google or Amazon do not rely on just one of these. They layer them. A request from a user might pass through a reverse proxy, then a firewall, then internal services accessed via secure channels, sometimes even over VPN-like tunnels between data centers.

The key takeaway is this: firewall protects, VPN secures, proxy intermediates. Each solves a different problem, and strong system design comes from combining them correctly rather than treating them as interchangeable.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Clustering and Replication
content:
When a user interacts with an application, it feels like data is simply written to a database and instantly available everywhere. But in reality, making the same data available across multiple machines reliably is a carefully engineered process called data replication.

Looking at the flow in the diagram, everything starts with the client sending a request to the application, which then reaches the server. The server writes the data to a primary database, often called the leader or master. This primary node is the single source of truth for all write operations. Every insert, update, or delete first happens here to maintain consistency.

Now comes the important part. Once the primary database accepts the write, it does not stop there. It propagates this change to multiple replica nodes. These replicas are essentially copies of the primary database, maintained to improve availability, scalability, and fault tolerance. The replication process can happen in two main ways: synchronous and asynchronous.

In synchronous replication, the primary waits for replicas to confirm that they have received and applied the change before acknowledging success to the client. This ensures strong consistency, meaning every read from any node returns the latest data. However, it comes at the cost of higher latency because the system must wait for multiple nodes.

In asynchronous replication, the primary immediately acknowledges the write after committing it locally and then sends updates to replicas in the background. This improves performance and reduces latency, but introduces eventual consistency. There is a small window where replicas might not have the latest data, which means a read from a replica could return slightly stale information.

Why not always use synchronous replication for perfect consistency?
Answer: because it slows down the system significantly, especially when replicas are geographically distributed. Most real-world systems choose a balance between performance and consistency.

Replication is usually implemented using logs. The primary database records every change in a write-ahead log or binary log. Replicas continuously read this log and apply the same operations in the same order. This ensures that replicas eventually reach the same state as the primary.

Another key concept is read scaling. Instead of sending all queries to the primary, systems often route read requests to replicas. This reduces load on the primary and allows the system to handle a much higher number of read operations. For example, in a social media app, writes (like posting content) go to the primary, while reads (like viewing posts) are served from replicas.

Failure handling is where replication truly proves its value. If the primary database goes down, one of the replicas can be promoted to become the new primary. This process is called failover. Modern systems automate this using leader election mechanisms to minimize downtime.

A quick question, what happens if replication lags behind significantly?
Answer: users might see outdated data, and in worst cases, failover can promote a replica that does not have the latest writes, leading to data loss. This is why monitoring replication lag is critical in production systems.

Real-world systems like MySQL, PostgreSQL, and distributed databases like Cassandra or MongoDB use variations of these replication strategies. Some use leader-follower models, while others use multi-leader or leaderless replication for even higher availability.

The key takeaway is this: data replication is not just about copying data, it is about maintaining consistency, ensuring availability, and enabling systems to scale. Every design decision, whether synchronous vs asynchronous or number of replicas, is a trade-off between performance, reliability, and correctness.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Clustering and Replication
content:
When a system runs on a single machine, life feels simple. There is one source of truth, one network boundary, and almost no ambiguity about where data lives or how it is accessed. But the moment a system becomes distributed, spread across multiple machines, racks, regions, or even continents, something fundamental changes. Communication is no longer guaranteed. And that is exactly where network partition enters the picture.

A network partition happens when a group of nodes in a distributed system loses the ability to communicate with another group. The nodes themselves are still alive, still processing, still holding data, but the “network glue” that keeps them in sync breaks temporarily. From the system’s perspective, this is not a rare edge case - it is a natural consequence of operating over unreliable networks.

If you think about how modern systems are built, data is constantly moving across machines through switches, routers, load balancers, and cables. Each of these components introduces a probability of failure. A cable can disconnect, a router can misroute packets, a cloud zone can go down, or latency can spike so high that nodes assume each other are unreachable. Even software issues like GC pauses or CPU throttling can mimic network failures by delaying responses long enough to trigger timeouts. In such an environment, expecting perfect communication is unrealistic.

Before a partition, all nodes appear synchronized. Data is replicated, requests are routed smoothly, and the system behaves like a single logical unit. But once the network splits, the system is effectively divided into isolated islands. One group of nodes can no longer see updates from the other. Now the system faces a critical decision: should it continue accepting requests independently in each partition, or should it stop operations to avoid inconsistency?

This is where the inevitability becomes more apparent. Distributed systems cannot prevent partitions—they can only decide how to behave when they occur. This tradeoff is formally captured in the CAP theorem, which states that in the presence of a network partition, a system must choose between consistency and availability. Either you ensure all nodes see the same data (consistency) by rejecting some requests, or you keep the system responsive (availability) by allowing divergence in data.

In real-world systems, partitions are not just theoretical. Large-scale systems frequently experience partial outages—one region becomes unreachable, a subset of services times out, or cross-data-center links degrade. Systems like distributed databases, message queues, and microservices architectures are all designed with the assumption that partitions will happen. Techniques like leader election, quorum reads/writes, replication strategies, and eventual consistency exist specifically to handle these scenarios.

Another subtle reason partitions are inevitable is scale. As the number of nodes increases, the probability of some subset failing to communicate grows significantly. What might be a rare event in a small cluster becomes a routine occurrence in a large distributed system. This is why companies operating at scale design for failure as a default assumption rather than an exception.

So the real takeaway is not how to avoid network partitions, but how to design systems that remain reliable despite them. Good system design embraces this reality by building fault-tolerant architectures, defining clear consistency models, and ensuring graceful degradation during failures. Because in distributed systems, the network is not just a medium - it is the weakest link, and it will fail.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
There is a common narrative in system design that microservices are always better. But that idea falls apart quickly when you look at how most successful systems actually begin. In many scenarios, a monolith is not just acceptable, it is the smarter and more efficient choice.

A monolithic architecture, as shown in the diagram, keeps everything together. The user interface, business logic, and data access layer are tightly integrated into a single codebase, directly connected to one database. This simplicity is often its biggest strength. There are no network calls between services, no distributed tracing, no complex deployment pipelines. Everything runs in one place, which makes development, debugging, and deployment significantly easier.

Monoliths shine especially in the early stages of a product. When requirements are unclear and features are evolving rapidly, you want speed over perfection. A monolith allows developers to move fast, iterate quickly, and make changes without worrying about breaking contracts between multiple services. Splitting into microservices too early often leads to over-engineering, where teams spend more time managing infrastructure than building features.

Another important scenario is small to medium scale systems. If your application does not yet face massive traffic or complex scaling needs, introducing distributed systems complexity does more harm than good. A monolith can handle a surprising amount of load with proper optimization, caching, and vertical scaling. Many companies run large workloads on well-structured monoliths for years before needing to break them apart.

We know that microservices scale better, then why not start with them from day one?
Answer: because scalability is not just about handling traffic, it is about managing complexity. Microservices introduce challenges like service discovery, network latency, data consistency, deployment coordination, and monitoring. If your system does not need that level of scalability yet, you are solving problems you do not have.

Monoliths are also easier to maintain for smaller teams. With fewer moving parts, coordination overhead is minimal. There is no need for multiple teams managing independent services, no versioning conflicts between APIs, and no distributed debugging headaches. A single team can understand and control the entire system.

Another underrated advantage is strong consistency. Since all components share the same database and run in the same process, transactions are straightforward. You do not have to deal with eventual consistency, distributed transactions, or complex failure scenarios that come with microservices.

However, this does not mean monoliths are always better. As systems grow, the codebase can become large and harder to manage. Scaling specific components independently becomes difficult, and deployment cycles may slow down. But the key idea is timing. A well-designed monolith can take you very far, and only when clear boundaries and scaling needs emerge should you consider breaking it into microservices.

The best systems are not built by blindly following trends, but by choosing the right architecture for the current stage of the product. In many cases, starting with a monolith and evolving gradually is not a compromise, it is a strategic advantage.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Kafka Internals: Topics and Partitions
content:
Everyone talks about Apache Kafka as the backbone of modern data systems, but very few stop to ask a deeper question - what happens when Kafka itself starts becoming the bottleneck at massive scale?

Kafka was designed around a simple but powerful idea: partition your data across brokers, replicate it for fault tolerance, and allow consumers to read sequential logs. This works beautifully for most systems. Producers push events into topics, those topics are split into partitions, and partitions are distributed across brokers. Replication ensures durability, and consumer groups enable parallel processing. For years, this model has powered event-driven architectures across companies like LinkedIn, Netflix, and Uber.

But now think from first principles. What happens when scale explodes to a level where partitions themselves become hard to manage? When rebalancing takes too long? When adding a new broker requires massive data movement? When hotspots form because some partitions receive disproportionately high traffic?

This is exactly where LinkedIn started rethinking things.

Instead of asking “how do we optimize Kafka,” they asked a more fundamental question: can we redesign the abstraction itself?

That’s where NorthGuard comes into the picture.

NorthGuard changes the way data distribution is handled. In Kafka, partitions are static units - once created, they define how data is distributed. But NorthGuard introduces a more flexible concept: segments within ranges. Instead of rigid partitions, data is broken into smaller segments that can be dynamically reassigned across brokers.

Now imagine what this means in practice. A new broker joins the cluster. In Kafka, you would need partition rebalancing, which involves moving large chunks of data and can impact performance. In NorthGuard, smaller segments can be reassigned incrementally. This reduces movement cost and makes scaling smoother.

Look at the diagram conceptually. Initially, brokers hold segments like S1, S2, S3 across a range. When a new broker is added, instead of reshuffling everything, a new segment (say S4) is introduced and distributed intelligently. Replication is maintained, but now at a finer granularity. This means better load balancing, reduced hotspots, and more efficient resource utilization.

Another key improvement is how replication works. Kafka replicates entire partitions, but NorthGuard can replicate at segment level. This gives more control and avoids unnecessary duplication of large datasets. It also helps in faster recovery during failures because smaller units are easier to rebuild and redistribute.

From a system design perspective, this is a shift from coarse-grained distribution to fine-grained distribution. And that one change unlocks multiple benefits: better scalability, faster rebalancing, improved fault tolerance, and more predictable performance under heavy load.

But here’s the important takeaway - NorthGuard is not a replacement for Kafka in every scenario. Kafka is still incredibly powerful and sufficient for the majority of systems. NorthGuard exists because LinkedIn operates at a scale where even small inefficiencies become massive problems.

So the real insight is not “Kafka vs NorthGuard.” The real insight is understanding when your system’s scale forces you to rethink core abstractions. Most systems don’t fail because of bad tools, they fail because the assumptions those tools were built on no longer hold true.

And that’s what makes this evolution interesting. It shows how distributed systems are not static designs, but continuously evolving ideas shaped by scale, constraints, and real-world pain points.

If you’re designing systems today, start simple. Use proven tools like Kafka. But always keep this in mind - as your scale grows, your architecture might need to evolve beyond the tools you started with.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Scalable Notification System Design
content:
CI/CD pipelines are the backbone of how modern software moves from a developer’s laptop to real users without chaos. At a high level, CI stands for Continuous Integration and CD stands for Continuous Delivery or Deployment. Together, they automate the journey of code so that every change is built, tested, and safely released with minimal manual effort.

Start from the first block in the flow. A developer commits changes to a repository. This is not just storing code, it is the trigger point of the entire pipeline. The moment code is pushed, an automated process kicks off. This is where Continuous Integration begins. The system detects the change and triggers a build. During the build phase, the application is compiled, dependencies are resolved, and artifacts are created. If something breaks here, the pipeline stops immediately, giving fast feedback to the developer.

Once the build is successful, the next stage is testing. Automated tests run to validate correctness. These include unit tests, integration tests, and sometimes even security checks. This step is critical because it ensures that new changes do not break existing functionality. If tests fail, the pipeline again stops, preventing faulty code from moving forward.

After tests pass, the pipeline moves toward delivery. The build artifact is deployed to a staging environment. This environment mimics production but is safer for validation. Teams can run additional checks, perform manual testing if needed, or verify system behavior under near-real conditions. Notifications are often sent at each stage—build success, test results, staging deployment—so teams stay informed without manually checking logs.

Finally comes deployment to production. If everything looks good, the system automatically pushes the changes to live users. In Continuous Delivery, this step may require manual approval. In Continuous Deployment, it happens automatically. The idea is simple: small, frequent, and reliable releases instead of large, risky ones.

Why not just deploy manually like earlier systems?
Answer: because manual deployments are slow, error-prone, and hard to scale. CI/CD removes human bottlenecks, ensures consistency, and allows teams to release features faster with confidence.

Another important aspect is feedback loops. Every stage in the pipeline provides immediate feedback. If a build fails, you know instantly. If a test breaks, you catch it before users do. This reduces debugging time and improves overall code quality.

From a system design perspective, CI/CD is not just a developer tool, it is a reliability mechanism. It enforces discipline in code changes, ensures reproducibility, and enables rapid iteration. Without it, scaling engineering teams becomes extremely difficult because coordination overhead increases.

Real-world systems use tools like Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or CircleCI to implement these pipelines. But the core idea remains the same: automate everything from commit to production while ensuring safety at each step.

The key takeaway is this: CI/CD pipelines transform software delivery from a manual, risky process into an automated, predictable flow. They allow teams to move fast without breaking things, which is exactly what modern system design demands.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Two Phase Locking (2PL) is one of those concepts in system d
content:
Two Phase Locking (2PL) is one of those concepts in system design that quietly ensures data consistency while multiple transactions are happening at the same time. At its core, it is a concurrency control mechanism used in databases to make sure that transactions do not interfere with each other in a way that corrupts data.

Think about what happens when multiple users try to update the same data simultaneously. Without any control, you can end up with race conditions, lost updates, or inconsistent reads. Two Phase Locking solves this by introducing a strict discipline on how locks are acquired and released during a transaction.

As shown in the flow, every transaction follows two distinct phases. The first phase is the growing phase. In this phase, the transaction is only allowed to acquire locks. It can take shared locks for reading or exclusive locks for writing, but it cannot release any lock yet. This phase continues until the transaction reaches its maximum lock point, also called the lock point. This is the moment when the transaction has acquired all the locks it will ever need.

After this point, the second phase begins, known as the shrinking phase. In this phase, the transaction starts releasing locks, and once it releases a lock, it is not allowed to acquire any new locks. This strict separation ensures that once a transaction starts letting go of resources, it cannot go back and request more, which prevents certain types of inconsistencies.

The key reason this works is because it guarantees conflict serializability. Even though transactions run concurrently, the end result is as if they were executed one after another in some order. This is critical in systems like banking, inventory management, or booking systems where correctness matters more than raw speed.

However, Two Phase Locking is not without trade-offs. One major issue is deadlocks. Imagine two transactions each holding a lock and waiting for the other to release theirs. Neither can proceed, and the system gets stuck. This is why real systems implement deadlock detection or prevention mechanisms on top of 2PL.

Another challenge is performance. Since locks restrict access, they can reduce concurrency. If many transactions are waiting on the same resource, throughput drops. To handle this, systems often use variations like Strict 2PL, where locks are held until the transaction commits, improving recoverability, or use optimizations like lock granularity and timeout strategies.

Then you might ask, why not just avoid locks altogether?
Answer: because without coordination, concurrent transactions can corrupt data. Locking introduces order in a chaotic environment, which is essential for maintaining correctness.

From a system design perspective, Two Phase Locking is about balancing consistency and concurrency. It provides strong guarantees but at the cost of complexity and potential performance overhead. That’s why modern systems sometimes move toward alternatives like optimistic concurrency control or MVCC, especially in high-scale distributed environments.

The big takeaway is simple: Two Phase Locking enforces a disciplined lifecycle for locks - first grow, then shrink and that discipline is what keeps concurrent systems correct and predictable.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
In system design, the “celebrity problem” is a metaphor for what happens when a single entity suddenly becomes extremely popular and attracts a disproportionate amount of traffic compared to everything else in the system. Think of a viral post, a trending product, or a famous user profile—millions of requests start hitting the same resource at the same time. Unlike normal traffic, which is usually distributed, celebrity traffic is highly skewed and concentrated, and that’s where problems begin.

Under normal conditions, systems are designed assuming relatively even distribution of load. Caches work efficiently, databases handle queries predictably, and load balancers distribute requests smoothly. But in a celebrity scenario, one key, one endpoint, or one piece of data becomes a hotspot. Suddenly, the cache for that item gets hammered, the database receives repeated reads or writes for the same row, and even upstream services start struggling because all requests are funneled toward a single dependency. This leads to increased latency, throttling, or even cascading failures.

The challenge becomes more interesting when writes are involved. For example, if a celebrity user posts something and millions of people start liking or commenting, the system now has to handle a massive number of concurrent writes to the same data. This can lead to lock contention, database bottlenecks, and inconsistent states if not handled properly. Even read-heavy scenarios are not safe—cache misses for a hot item can trigger a “thundering herd” problem where multiple requests hit the database simultaneously before the cache is populated.

So how do systems handle this? One common approach is aggressive caching. Frequently accessed data is stored in distributed caches so that repeated reads don’t hit the database. But caching alone is not enough. Techniques like request coalescing are used to ensure that multiple identical requests are combined into one backend call. Rate limiting can also be applied to protect the system from overload.

Another important strategy is replication and sharding. By replicating data across multiple nodes, read traffic can be distributed. For writes, systems may use techniques like batching, queues, or eventual consistency models to reduce immediate pressure. In some cases, counters (like likes or views) are handled using specialized systems that allow approximate or delayed updates instead of strict real-time accuracy.

Then why is this problem hard if we already have load balancers?
Answer: because load balancers distribute requests across servers, but they cannot distribute load within a single data item. If all requests target the same key, the bottleneck shifts deeper into the system—usually the database or cache layer.

From a system design perspective, the celebrity problem teaches an important lesson: average load is not the real challenge, skewed load is. Systems must be designed not just for scale, but for uneven scale. Handling the “hottest” part of your system efficiently often determines overall reliability.

In short, the celebrity problem is about dealing with extreme hotspots in traffic and ensuring that one popular entity does not bring down the entire system. Designing for it means thinking beyond uniform distribution and preparing for sudden, concentrated spikes in demand.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
Choosing the right database is not about picking what’s popular, it’s about understanding your application’s behavior and matching it with the database’s strengths. The biggest mistake people make is starting with a database choice before clearly defining requirements. Instead, start from first principles: what kind of data do you have, how it is accessed, and how it will scale over time.

The first question to ask is about data structure. If your data is highly structured with clear relationships, like transactions, orders, and users, a relational database like PostgreSQL or MySQL makes sense because it enforces schema and supports joins. But if your data is flexible, nested, or evolving frequently, a NoSQL database like MongoDB is often a better fit since it allows schema flexibility.

Next comes access patterns, which matter more than data itself. Ask yourself: are you doing heavy reads, heavy writes, or a mix of both? If your system is read-heavy, caching layers and databases optimized for fast lookups like Redis can drastically improve performance. If you are handling high write throughput, like logging or event ingestion, systems like Apache Cassandra are designed to scale writes horizontally with minimal latency.

Consistency requirements are another critical factor. If your application cannot tolerate inconsistencies, such as in banking or payment systems, you need strong consistency guarantees, which traditional relational databases provide. But if your system can tolerate slight delays in consistency, like social media feeds or analytics dashboards, eventually consistent systems can give you better scalability and availability.

Scalability is where many decisions change. Vertical scaling (adding more power to a single machine) works well initially and is simpler, but it has limits. Horizontal scaling (adding more machines) requires distributed databases. Systems like Cassandra or DynamoDB are built for this from the ground up, while relational databases can scale horizontally but with more complexity.

Another important dimension is query complexity. If your application relies heavily on complex queries, joins, aggregations, and reporting, relational databases are hard to beat. But if your queries are simple key-value lookups or document retrievals, NoSQL databases will be faster and easier to scale.

Can one database solve everything?
Answer: rarely. Most real-world systems use a combination of databases, known as polyglot persistence. For example, a system might use PostgreSQL for transactions, Redis for caching, and Elasticsearch for search.

Operational complexity also matters. Some databases are easy to set up but hard to scale, while others require more initial effort but handle growth better. You should consider factors like backup, replication, failover, and monitoring. A database that fits your use case but is hard to operate can become a bottleneck later.

Finally, think about future growth. Your current scale might not justify a distributed database, but your design should not block you from evolving. Start simple, but choose technologies that allow migration or extension when needed.

The key takeaway is this: there is no “best database,” only the most suitable one for your specific problem. The right decision comes from understanding your data, access patterns, consistency needs, and scalability goals, not from following trends.

Happy designing ❤️

title: At first glance,  Elasticsearch feels like a very fast datab
content:
At first glance,  Elasticsearch feels like a very fast database for searching text. But internally, it works very differently from traditional databases. Instead of scanning rows one by one like SQL systems often do, Elasticsearch builds highly optimized search indexes that allow it to find matching documents almost instantly, even among billions of records.

The core idea behind Elasticsearch is the inverted index. In a normal database, data is stored document by document. But Elasticsearch flips this structure. Instead of storing “document → words,” it stores “word → documents.” This is why it is called an inverted index.

For example, imagine three documents:
* “Distributed systems are hard”
* “System design is important”
* “Distributed databases scale horizontally”

Elasticsearch breaks sentences into tokens (words), processes them, and creates an index like:
* distributed → doc1, doc3
* system → doc2
* systems → doc1
* databases → doc3

Now when a user searches for “distributed,” Elasticsearch directly jumps to the matching documents instead of scanning every record. This is what makes searches extremely fast.

The process starts with indexing. When data enters Elasticsearch, it first goes through analyzers. These analyzers tokenize text, remove stop words, normalize casing, and sometimes apply stemming. For example, “Running,” “runs,” and “run” may all become “run.” This preprocessing improves search quality.

Once analyzed, the document is stored inside an index. An index in Elasticsearch is similar to a database in SQL terminology. But internally, indexes are split into shards. Shards are smaller partitions of data distributed across multiple nodes in a cluster. This is how Elasticsearch scales horizontally.

Suppose an index contains 1 billion documents. Instead of storing everything on one machine, Elasticsearch divides the index into shards and spreads them across servers. Queries are executed in parallel on multiple shards, making searches much faster. Replicas are also created for fault tolerance. If one node goes down, another replica shard can continue serving requests.

The search flow itself is interesting. When a query arrives, Elasticsearch sends the query to relevant shards. Each shard independently computes the best matches and returns results. Then Elasticsearch combines these partial results, ranks them using relevance scoring algorithms like TF-IDF or BM25, and returns the final ordered response.

This relevance scoring is why Elasticsearch is powerful for full-text search. It does not just check if a word exists - it calculates how important that word is inside the document relative to all other documents. A document containing the searched term multiple times may rank higher than others.
