Another major reason Elasticsearch is popular is near real-time search. Newly indexed documents become searchable within seconds. Internally, Elasticsearch uses segments and periodic refresh operations instead of rewriting the entire index continuously. This design balances speed and efficiency.

Then why not use SQL LIKE queries instead?
Answer: because LIKE queries become extremely slow at scale. Searching through billions of rows using string matching requires scanning huge amounts of data. Elasticsearch avoids this using inverted indexes optimized specifically for search workloads.

Elasticsearch is also heavily distributed by design. Cluster managers coordinate nodes, shards move automatically during failures, and replicas ensure high availability. This makes it suitable for log analytics, observability platforms, recommendation systems, e-commerce search, and real-time monitoring systems.

But Elasticsearch is not meant to replace primary databases entirely. It is optimized for search and analytics, not for strict ACID transactions. This is why many systems use it alongside relational databases. Data is stored in the main database and then synchronized into Elasticsearch for fast querying.

In simple terms, Elasticsearch works by converting text into searchable indexes, distributing those indexes across clusters, and executing parallel relevance-based searches extremely efficiently. Instead of searching documents directly, it searches indexes built specifically for speed.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
Imagine opening a social media app and seeing that the platform is completely down because the system is trying to maintain perfect consistency across all servers. Users cannot scroll feeds, upload stories, or send messages. From a business perspective, this is disastrous because social media platforms depend heavily on continuous interaction and user retention. Even a few minutes of downtime can lead to massive traffic loss, reduced engagement, and revenue impact.

Now compare that with a consistency issue. Suppose you upload a photo and your follower sees it after 5 seconds instead of instantly. Or your like count shows 100 on one device and 102 on another for a short period of time. Most users will not even notice these inconsistencies because they are temporary and eventually corrected. This is why social media platforms often prefer eventual consistency over strict consistency.

This trade-off becomes clearer when viewed through the CAP theorem. In distributed systems, during a network partition, you must choose between consistency and availability. Social media platforms typically prioritize availability because user interactions should continue even if some parts of the system are temporarily out of sync. If one data center cannot communicate with another, the system still allows users to browse feeds, post content, and interact, while synchronization happens later in the background.

Another important reason is scale. Social media applications operate globally with users spread across continents. Data replication across regions introduces latency. If every write operation required strict synchronization across all replicas before responding to the user, performance would become extremely slow. By relaxing consistency requirements, systems can respond faster and provide a smoother experience.

Feeds themselves are naturally tolerant to inconsistency. A feed is already personalized, ranked, cached, and dynamically generated. Showing slightly outdated data rarely harms functionality. In fact, many systems intentionally use caching and asynchronous updates to improve responsiveness, even though it introduces temporary inconsistencies.

Are there places in social media where consistency still matters?
Answer: absolutely. Payment systems, authentication, ad billing, and security-related operations still require strong consistency because incorrect data there can cause financial or security problems.

Modern social media systems therefore use selective consistency. Critical operations use strong guarantees, while high-scale user interactions favor availability and low latency. This hybrid approach allows platforms to remain fast, scalable, and resilient under enormous traffic.

The key takeaway is simple: in social media, users care more about the platform being accessible and responsive than about every piece of data being perfectly synchronized at every moment. Availability keeps the platform alive, while eventual consistency quietly fixes temporary mismatches in the background.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
At first, storing images and videos directly inside a database sounds convenient. Everything stays in one place - user data, posts, media, metadata. But in real-world large-scale systems, this quickly becomes a major performance and scalability problem. That is why modern architectures store media files in object storage systems like Amazon S3 or Google Cloud Storage, while the database only stores metadata and file URLs.

In the diagram, the user uploads media through the frontend application. The backend server receives the request, but instead of inserting the actual image or video binary into the database, it uploads the media to object storage. The database only stores lightweight information such as the media URL, file type, owner ID, timestamps, captions, and permissions.

The first reason is size. Images and especially videos are extremely large compared to normal database records. A user profile row might be only a few kilobytes, but a single HD video can be hundreds of megabytes or even gigabytes. Databases are optimized for structured transactional data, not for storing massive binary blobs repeatedly. If media files are stored directly in the database, database size grows uncontrollably, backups become huge, replication slows down, and query performance degrades.

The second reason is scalability. Social media and streaming platforms handle billions of media files. Object storage systems are specifically designed for this use case. Systems like Amazon S3 can scale almost infinitely, distribute files globally, and provide high durability automatically. Traditional relational databases are not built to efficiently serve millions of large file downloads simultaneously.

Another major issue is database performance. Databases work best when memory caches can hold frequently accessed indexes and records. Large binary files pollute this cache. Instead of caching useful query data, the system wastes memory handling huge media blobs. This increases latency for normal operations like login, search, or fetching user feeds.

Replication also becomes expensive if media is stored in the database. Distributed databases replicate data across nodes for fault tolerance. Replicating terabytes or petabytes of video data between replicas creates enormous network overhead and storage costs. Object storage systems already solve replication efficiently at the infrastructure layer, so duplicating this work inside databases is unnecessary.

Content delivery is another important factor. Media files are usually served through CDNs (Content Delivery Networks). Object storage integrates naturally with CDNs, allowing users to fetch content from edge servers near their location. This reduces latency and bandwidth usage on backend servers. If media were served directly from databases, databases would become bottlenecks under high traffic.

Then why store URLs in the database at all?
Answer: because applications still need metadata and relationships. The database stores references to the media so the application knows which image belongs to which user, post, or comment.

Object storage also provides lifecycle management features. Old files can automatically move to cheaper cold storage tiers, archives, or backups. Databases are not optimized for such storage tiering mechanisms.

There is also operational simplicity. Backing up a transactional database containing only metadata is much faster and cheaper than backing up enormous multimedia blobs. Disaster recovery becomes simpler because application state and media storage are separated.

Some databases technically support BLOB storage, and for small systems or tiny files it can work. But once systems scale, separating transactional data from media storage becomes almost mandatory.

In modern system design, databases are treated as systems for structured relationships and fast queries, while object storage systems are treated as durable scalable repositories for large unstructured files. This separation allows each component to specialize in what it does best.

The key idea is simple: databases manage information about files, while object storage manages the files themselves.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Kafka Fundamentals and Event Streaming
content:
Choosing a tech stack is one of the most important decisions in system design because every layer of the application depends on it. A good tech stack can help a product scale smoothly for years, while a bad decision can create performance bottlenecks, operational complexity, and expensive migrations later. The biggest mistake developers make is choosing technologies based on hype instead of actual system requirements.

In the diagram, the left side shows the layered architecture of a typical application stack - User Interface, Services, Middleware, Database, Operating System, and Hardware/Network. The right side shows examples of technologies that can exist at each layer. Every application is basically a combination of choices made across these layers.

The first step in deciding a tech stack is understanding the problem deeply. Different systems have completely different requirements. A banking application prioritizes consistency and security. A social media platform prioritizes availability and scalability. A live-streaming platform prioritizes low latency and throughput. An AI application may prioritize GPU computation and model serving. The architecture always starts from requirements, not technologies.

Frontend technologies are selected based on user experience and platform requirements. Web applications usually use frameworks like React, Angular, or Vue because they support component-based rendering and interactive UI updates efficiently. Mobile applications may use Kotlin, Swift, Flutter, or React Native depending on whether native performance or cross-platform development speed matters more.

The backend stack depends heavily on concurrency patterns and workload type. Node.js is often selected for I/O-heavy systems because of its asynchronous event-driven model. Go is preferred in many distributed systems because it handles concurrency efficiently with goroutines while consuming less memory. Java remains dominant in enterprise systems because of its mature ecosystem, strong reliability, and excellent tooling. Python is heavily used in AI systems due to its machine learning ecosystem.

Database selection is one of the hardest decisions in system design. Relational databases like PostgreSQL and MySQL are chosen when transactions, joins, and strict consistency are important. NoSQL databases like MongoDB or Cassandra are preferred when horizontal scalability and flexible schemas matter more. Redis is used for caching and low-latency lookups. Elasticsearch is added when full-text search becomes critical. The database is selected based on access patterns, not popularity.

Middleware and communication layers become important as systems scale. REST APIs work well for standard client-server communication, but gRPC is often used for low-latency internal service communication. Kafka is introduced when systems need event streaming, asynchronous processing, and high-throughput messaging. RabbitMQ is preferred in workflows requiring reliable queues and message acknowledgments.

Infrastructure decisions also influence the stack heavily. Small startups may initially deploy on a single VM because operational simplicity matters more than scalability. As traffic grows, containerization with Docker and orchestration through Kubernetes may become necessary. Cloud providers like AWS, Azure, and GCP reduce operational burden through managed services such as databases, storage, queues, and autoscaling infrastructure.

One important factor people ignore is team expertise. A theoretically perfect stack becomes useless if the engineering team cannot debug or maintain it efficiently. Sometimes a simpler and more familiar stack leads to faster delivery and fewer production failures than a highly optimized but complex architecture.

Scalability expectations also change stack decisions. If an application serves only a few thousand users, introducing microservices, Kubernetes, distributed tracing, and event-driven architectures may create unnecessary complexity. In many cases, a modular monolith is actually the better engineering choice during early stages.

Cost is another hidden factor. Some technologies scale technically but become financially expensive at high traffic. Engineers must think not only about performance but also infrastructure costs, operational overhead, cloud pricing, and long-term maintainability.

Security and compliance requirements also affect stack selection. Healthcare and banking systems often use mature ecosystems with strong auditing and compliance tooling. AI startups may prioritize experimentation speed instead of strict enterprise-grade stability.

Another major consideration is future extensibility. Good tech stacks allow systems to evolve gradually. A tightly coupled stack may work initially but become difficult to modify as business requirements grow. Engineers usually prefer technologies with strong ecosystems, community support, observability tooling, and long-term stability.

The most important lesson in system design is that there is no universally “best” tech stack. Every stack is a tradeoff between scalability, speed, consistency, operational complexity, developer productivity, and cost.

The best system designers do not start with technologies.
They start with constraints.

Once constraints become clear, the correct tech stack usually becomes obvious naturally.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Chaos Engineering is the practice of intentionally breaking
content:
Chaos Engineering is the practice of intentionally breaking parts of a distributed system in a controlled way to understand how the system behaves during failures. At first this sounds dangerous because engineers are deliberately injecting failures into production-like environments. But modern large-scale systems are already full of unpredictable failures - servers crash, databases become slow, networks partition, APIs timeout, disks fail, and traffic suddenly spikes. Chaos Engineering exists because failures are not rare events in distributed systems. Failures are guaranteed to happen eventually.

In the diagram, the process starts from a “steady state.” This means the system is operating normally and engineers first define what “healthy” actually means. It could be request latency, successful payments, streaming uptime, or API success rate. Without a measurable steady state, engineers cannot determine whether the system survived the experiment successfully.

After defining the steady state, engineers design a chaos experiment and create a hypothesis. The hypothesis is basically an assumption about how the system should behave during failure. For example:
“If one service instance crashes, traffic should automatically shift to healthy replicas without affecting users.”

This step is extremely important because Chaos Engineering is not random destruction. It is a scientific process based on measurable expectations.

The next stage is minimizing the blast radius. Engineers never start by shutting down an entire production region immediately. Instead, failures are introduced gradually in small isolated environments or a tiny subset of traffic. The goal is learning safely without causing large-scale outages. This controlled failure approach allows teams to identify weak points before real disasters happen.

Then comes fault injection, which is the core of Chaos Engineering. Engineers intentionally inject failures into the system. This can include shutting down servers, increasing network latency, corrupting packets, exhausting CPU or memory, killing Kubernetes pods, simulating database failures, or disconnecting services from each other. Netflix became one of the most famous adopters of this idea through Chaos Monkey, which randomly terminated production instances to ensure systems were resilient by design.

Once failures are injected, engineers analyse system metrics carefully. This stage checks whether autoscaling triggered correctly, whether failover worked, whether retries caused overload, whether caches protected databases, and whether alerts fired properly. Sometimes systems that appear stable under normal conditions completely collapse during cascading failures because hidden dependencies were never tested earlier.

The diagram then moves into learning from the experiment and fixing failures. This is where the actual value of Chaos Engineering appears. The goal is not simply breaking systems. The goal is discovering weaknesses before users discover them first. Engineers may realize that a retry mechanism creates traffic storms, replicas are not syncing correctly, load balancers fail slowly, or circuit breakers are missing entirely.

After fixes are implemented, engineers validate whether the original hypothesis was correct. If the system behaves as expected even during faults, the architecture becomes more resilient over time. Chaos Engineering slowly transforms systems from “working under ideal conditions” into “surviving under real-world failures.”

One important lesson in distributed systems is that redundancy alone does not guarantee resilience. Many systems have replicas, backups, and failover nodes, but nobody truly knows whether they work correctly until failures actually happen. Chaos Engineering exposes these hidden operational gaps.

This practice becomes especially important in cloud-native architectures because microservices create thousands of network interactions between independent services. Even if individual services are reliable, the overall distributed system may still fail unpredictably due to dependency chains and cascading failures.

Modern companies like Netflix, Amazon, Google, Meta, and Uber heavily invest in Chaos Engineering because their systems operate at global scale where failures are statistically unavoidable. Instead of fearing failures, they continuously rehearse them.

The biggest mindset shift is this:
Traditional testing checks whether systems work.
Chaos Engineering checks whether systems survive.

In distributed systems, survival matters more than perfection.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Kafka Fundamentals and Event Streaming
content:
Most freshers think system design is only for senior engineers or architects working on massive distributed systems. But in reality, learning system design early completely changes the way you think about software engineering. It shifts your mindset from “writing code that works” to “building systems that survive, scale, and evolve.”

As a fresher, most coding problems revolve around algorithms, syntax, frameworks, and project implementation. These are important because they teach logic and development fundamentals. But real-world software engineering is much larger than writing functions or APIs. Once applications start getting real users, completely different problems appear — scalability, latency, reliability, caching, replication, load balancing, failover, database bottlenecks, monitoring, and cost optimization. System design teaches you how all these pieces connect together.

For example, building a simple chat app in college is easy. But designing a chat system for millions of concurrent users is a completely different engineering challenge. Suddenly you need message queues, WebSockets, distributed databases, caching layers, replication strategies, CDN usage, rate limiting, and fault tolerance. System design helps you understand why these components exist in the first place.

One of the biggest advantages of learning system design early is that it improves your backend thinking automatically. Even while building small projects, you start asking better engineering questions:
What happens if traffic suddenly spikes?
What if the database goes down?
Can this service scale horizontally?
Should this data be cached?
How do retries affect the system?
Where can bottlenecks appear?

This thought process makes your projects look far more mature than normal CRUD applications.

Another important reason is interviews. Many companies now ask low-level or high-level design questions even for internships and entry-level roles. They may not expect deep distributed systems expertise from freshers, but they definitely value engineers who can think beyond syntax and understand architectural tradeoffs.

Learning system design also helps you understand modern technologies much faster. Tools like Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, ElasticSearch, API Gateways, Load Balancers, CDNs, and Docker stop looking like random buzzwords. You begin understanding the actual problem each technology solves.

For example:
Redis exists because databases alone become slow under repeated reads.
Kafka exists because direct service-to-service communication becomes unreliable at scale.
CDNs exist because serving global traffic from one region increases latency.
Replication exists because hardware failures are inevitable.

Once you understand first principles, technologies become easier to learn because you understand the “why” behind them.

System design also teaches one of the most important engineering lessons:
Every architectural decision is a tradeoff.

There is no “perfect architecture.”
Microservices improve scalability but increase operational complexity.
Caching improves speed but introduces consistency issues.
Replication improves availability but makes synchronization harder.
Sharding improves scalability but complicates querying.

Freshers who learn this early become better decision makers instead of blindly copying tech stacks from large companies.

Another underrated benefit is communication skills. System design forces you to explain flows, bottlenecks, and architecture clearly. In industry, engineering impact is not only about coding speed. Engineers constantly discuss tradeoffs, propose architectures, explain failures, and collaborate across teams. System design naturally improves this ability.

Even startups value this mindset heavily. Small teams need engineers who can think holistically instead of only implementing assigned tickets. A fresher who understands scalability, reliability, and architecture often stands out quickly.

Most importantly, system design makes software engineering feel real. Instead of seeing applications as isolated code files, you start seeing them as living systems interacting through networks, databases, queues, caches, storage layers, and distributed infrastructure.

You stop asking:
“How do I code this feature?”

And start asking:
“How will this feature behave under real-world scale and failures?”

That mindset transition is exactly what transforms developers into engineers.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Clustering and Replication
content:
At first glance, scaling a database sounds simple -  just add more servers when traffic increases. But traditional relational databases struggle here because scaling usually means vertical scaling: adding more CPU, memory, or storage to a single machine. Eventually, hardware limits are reached. This is exactly the problem databases like  Apache Cassandra were designed to solve.

Cassandra was built for systems where downtime is unacceptable and traffic grows unpredictably across multiple regions. Instead of relying on one powerful server, Cassandra distributes data across many nodes and scales horizontally almost infinitely by simply adding more machines to the cluster.

The core idea behind Cassandra scaling is decentralization. There is no master node controlling the cluster. Every node is equal and capable of handling reads and writes independently. This removes single points of failure and allows the database to continue operating even if multiple nodes crash simultaneously.

Internally, Cassandra uses consistent hashing to distribute data across nodes. Each piece of data is assigned a partition key, and a hash function determines which node should store that data. In the hash ring architecture, nodes are responsible for specific token ranges.

For example:
* User A’s data may go to Node 1
* User B’s data may go to Node 4
* User C’s data may go to Node 7

As traffic increases, new nodes can simply join the ring and take ownership of some token ranges. Existing nodes automatically rebalance data to the new node. This is one of the reasons Cassandra scales smoothly without major downtime.

Replication is another major reason Cassandra scales reliably. Data is not stored on only one machine. Multiple replicas are maintained across different nodes and even across regions. If one node goes down, another replica can immediately serve requests.

This replication model also improves read scalability because read traffic can be distributed across replicas instead of overloading a single machine.

One interesting thing about Cassandra is tunable consistency. Applications can decide how many replicas must acknowledge reads or writes before an operation is considered successful.

For example:
* ONE → fastest but weaker consistency
* QUORUM → balanced consistency and availability
* ALL → strongest consistency but slower and less fault tolerant

This flexibility allows Cassandra to optimize differently for different workloads.

Scaling writes is where Cassandra becomes extremely powerful. Cassandra is optimized for high write throughput because writes are append-heavy and sequential internally. Incoming writes first go to a commit log for durability and then into an in-memory structure called a memtable. Later, memtables are flushed to immutable SSTables on disk.

Because Cassandra avoids random disk updates frequently, it handles massive write workloads efficiently. This is why companies handling IoT data, messaging systems, logs, analytics streams, and time-series workloads often use Cassandra.

Another important reason Cassandra scales well is peer-to-peer communication. Nodes exchange cluster information using a gossip protocol. Instead of depending on a centralized coordinator, nodes continuously share metadata about cluster health and topology with each other. This makes failure detection distributed and resilient.

The diagram of a Cassandra cluster usually shows multiple nodes distributed across racks and regions. This physical separation matters because Cassandra was designed with fault tolerance in mind. Entire racks or data centers can fail while the system still remains operational.

But scaling Cassandra also introduces tradeoffs.

One major challenge is eventual consistency. Since replicas synchronize asynchronously in many configurations, different nodes may temporarily return slightly different versions of data. Cassandra prioritizes availability and partition tolerance heavily, especially under network failures.

Data modeling is also very different compared to relational databases. Cassandra queries must usually be designed around access patterns beforehand because joins and complex aggregations are limited.

Compaction is another operational challenge. Since Cassandra stores immutable SSTables, background compaction processes merge files continuously. Poor compaction tuning can create performance problems at large scale.

Despite these tradeoffs, Cassandra remains one of the most powerful databases for horizontally scalable distributed workloads. It was originally developed at Facebook to handle inbox search and messaging workloads where massive scalability and availability were more important than strict transactional consistency.

The biggest philosophy behind Cassandra is simple:
Instead of building one giant database server,
build many smaller distributed nodes that cooperate together.

That is how systems survive internet-scale traffic.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Clustering and Replication
content:
At first glance, Telegram looks like a simple messaging application where users send texts, images, videos, and files to each other. But internally, Telegram operates as a massive distributed real-time communication system handling millions of concurrent connections, message deliveries, media uploads, push notifications, and synchronization across multiple devices simultaneously.

In the diagram, the request flow starts from the client device. Every request first passes through AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall). This layer protects the infrastructure from malicious traffic such as DDoS attacks, bot abuse, spam requests, and API exploitation attempts. Since messaging platforms are public-facing systems exposed to the internet continuously, security filtering at the edge becomes extremely important.

After WAF, traffic moves into AWS CloudFront, which acts as a CDN and edge acceleration layer. Static assets, profile images, media thumbnails, and cached content can be delivered from servers geographically close to users. This reduces latency globally and prevents backend servers from becoming overloaded with repeated content requests.

Next, requests reach the Load Balancer. This is one of the most important layers in large-scale distributed systems. Telegram cannot rely on a single backend server because millions of users may be active simultaneously. The load balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple backend instances to ensure no single machine becomes a bottleneck.

Behind the load balancer, the architecture is divided into multiple microservices. Instead of one giant monolithic backend handling everything, Telegram separates responsibilities into specialized services.

The User Service handles user-related operations such as profile management, authentication, usernames, account settings, and contact information. Isolating this logic helps scale user management independently from messaging workloads.

The Session Service manages active user sessions and device synchronization. One of Telegram’s core features is multi-device access where the same account works simultaneously across phones, desktops, tablets, and browsers. This requires session coordination and state synchronization across distributed infrastructure.

The Cloud Service handles media storage and cloud synchronization. Telegram supports enormous file uploads, cloud-based chats, and persistent media history. Storing all this directly inside transactional databases would become extremely inefficient, so object storage systems are usually involved internally for scalable media handling.

The Last Seen Service tracks user activity status such as online presence, typing indicators, active sessions, and timestamps. This may sound small, but presence systems are extremely difficult at scale because millions of users constantly change state in real time.

One of the most important components in the diagram is the Message Queue Service. Messaging platforms cannot process everything synchronously because traffic spikes can become unpredictable. Queues help decouple producers from consumers. Messages are temporarily stored inside distributed queues before being processed asynchronously by downstream systems. This prevents sudden traffic bursts from crashing services.

The Chat Service is the core communication engine. It handles message delivery, chat synchronization, group management, encryption coordination, message ordering, read receipts, and media attachments. In distributed messaging systems, maintaining correct message order while scaling globally is one of the hardest engineering problems.

The Push Notification Service ensures users receive alerts even when the application is closed. This service integrates with Android FCM, Apple APNS, and web notification systems. Push systems are highly optimized because billions of notifications may need delivery daily.

On the right side of the diagram, every service connects to its own database. This is a common microservice architecture pattern called database-per-service design. Instead of sharing one giant database across all services, each service owns its data independently. This improves scalability, fault isolation, and deployment flexibility.

For example:
* Chat Service may use distributed databases optimized for write-heavy workloads.
* Session Service may use Redis-like in-memory storage for fast lookups.
* Media systems may use object storage.
* Notification systems may use queue-oriented storage.

This separation prevents one overloaded subsystem from affecting the entire platform.

Another major challenge Telegram solves is real-time communication. Millions of users maintain long-lived TCP or WebSocket-like persistent connections simultaneously. Efficient connection handling becomes critical because each open connection consumes server memory and resources.

Replication and fault tolerance are also extremely important. Messaging platforms cannot afford large outages because communication systems are expected to remain available continuously. Data is replicated across regions and multiple servers to ensure high availability during failures.

The most interesting thing about Telegram’s architecture is that it combines:
* Real-time systems
* Distributed databases
* Queue-based asynchronous processing
* Global CDN distribution
* Multi-device synchronization
* Massive fanout messaging
* Fault-tolerant infrastructure

All together into one continuously operating distributed system.

What looks like “just sending a message”
is actually one of the most complex large-scale system design problems on the internet.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Scalable Notification System Design
content:
At first, the Subscribe button on YouTube looked sufficient. If a user subscribed to a creator, then naturally they should receive updates whenever that creator uploads a new video. But as YouTube scaled to billions of users and millions of creators, this simple model started creating one of the biggest notification and distributed systems challenges on the internet. That is exactly why YouTube introduced the Bell icon.

The Bell icon was not just a UI improvement.
It was actually an infrastructure optimization hidden behind a product feature.

Initially, subscriptions represented user interest. But over time, user behavior changed dramatically. People started subscribing to hundreds of channels — music, gaming, podcasts, education, shorts, livestreams, tech reviews, and entertainment. Now imagine if YouTube sent a push notification for every upload from every subscribed channel.

Phones would become notification spam machines.

Now think about this problem from a system design perspective.

Suppose a creator like MrBeast uploads a new video.
Tens of millions of users may be subscribed to the channel.

The moment the upload happens, YouTube’s backend potentially has to:

* Generate millions of notification events
* Queue notifications globally
* Push alerts to Android, iOS, web, and email systems
* Handle retries for failed deliveries
* Respect mobile OS rate limits
* Prevent notification storms
* Prioritize active devices
* Track delivery analytics

This becomes an enormous fanout problem.

A single upload from a massive creator can create traffic spikes across notification systems, queues, databases, recommendation engines, and push delivery services simultaneously.

The Subscribe button alone was too broad.

So YouTube separated two different user intents:

Subscribe →
“I want this creator in my ecosystem.”

Bell Icon →
“I want immediate high-priority alerts.”

This distinction is extremely important.

Most subscribers are passive consumers. They may watch videos occasionally through homepage recommendations, trending feeds, or search results. They do not necessarily want interruptions every time a creator uploads content.

But Bell users are highly engaged users.
These users explicitly opt into real-time delivery.

This changes backend behavior significantly.

Instead of sending expensive push notifications to all subscribers, YouTube can aggressively notify only the subset of users who enabled the Bell icon. This dramatically reduces:

* Push infrastructure load
* Queue traffic
* Retry overhead
* Device wakeups
* Notification delivery costs
* User fatigue

In distributed systems terms, the Bell icon acts like a filtering layer before massive fanout operations happen.

Another hidden reason involves recommendation systems.

YouTube does not blindly distribute videos equally to everyone. When a video uploads, recommendation algorithms first observe engagement signals:

* Click-through rate
* Watch time
* Retention
* Likes/comments/shares
* Session continuation

If the content performs well, YouTube gradually amplifies distribution.

But Bell users bypass some of this uncertainty because they already declared strong interest manually. That makes notification delivery more efficient and predictable.

The Bell icon also helps creator analytics.

Without it, creators would complain:
“Subscribers are not seeing my videos.”

Now YouTube can differentiate:

* Casual subscribers
* Personalized notification users
* All-notification users

This gives creators more visibility into actual audience engagement quality instead of raw subscriber counts.

There is also an important mobile systems perspective here.

Push notifications are expensive at scale.
Every notification may wake devices, consume battery, trigger network requests, and create backend synchronization events. Sending unnecessary notifications to billions of devices daily becomes both technically and economically inefficient.

The Bell icon reduces this unnecessary distributed traffic.

Another interesting engineering insight is prioritization.

Large-scale systems cannot treat all users equally all the time.
Infrastructure resources are finite.

So platforms create layers of intent:

* Passive engagement
* Active engagement
* Real-time engagement

The Bell icon essentially marks users as high-priority consumers for real-time delivery systems.

What looks like a tiny product feature to users
is actually a large-scale distributed systems optimization internally.

The most fascinating part of system design is this:
Sometimes the best infrastructure solution appears as a simple UI button.

Happy designing ❤️

title: At first, deploying software manually sounds manageable
content:
At first, deploying software manually sounds manageable. A developer writes code, creates a build, runs tests locally, uploads files to servers, restarts services, and the application goes live. This works for tiny systems with very few deployments. But once applications scale, teams grow, and releases happen multiple times a day, manual deployment becomes one of the biggest bottlenecks in software engineering.

That is why modern distributed systems rely on CI/CD pipelines.

CI/CD stands for:
* Continuous Integration (CI)
* Continuous Delivery / Continuous Deployment (CD)

The goal is simple:
automate the entire software delivery lifecycle from code commit to production deployment.
In a real-world system design architecture, a CI/CD pipeline acts like an automated assembly line for software.

The process usually starts when a developer pushes code changes to a Git repository such as GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. This commit becomes the trigger event for the pipeline.

The first stage is *Continuous Integration* .
As soon as the commit happens, the CI server automatically starts a build pipeline. Tools like Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, or Azure DevOps continuously monitor repositories for new changes.

The pipeline first pulls the latest code and starts dependency installation. Libraries, packages, modules, and required runtimes are prepared inside isolated build environments.

Next comes *compilation* and *build generation* .
For example:
* Java projects generate JAR/WAR files
* React applications create optimized frontend bundles
* Docker-based systems build container images
* Go services generate static binaries

The purpose of this stage is to ensure the codebase can successfully build after every commit.

After the build stage, *automated testing* begins.
This is one of the most important parts of CI/CD pipelines because testing acts as the first defense against bad deployments.

Different types of tests usually run:
* Unit tests
* Integration tests
* API tests
* UI tests
* Regression tests
* Security scans
* Performance checks

If any critical test fails, the pipeline immediately stops. This prevents broken code from reaching production systems.

In large organizations, thousands of builds may run daily. Automated testing ensures engineers do not manually verify every deployment repeatedly.

Once tests pass successfully, the pipeline moves toward *Continuous Delivery* .

Now the system prepares deployment artifacts such as:
* Docker images
* Kubernetes manifests
* Helm charts
* Binary packages
* Infrastructure templates

These artifacts are usually stored in artifact repositories like:
* Docker Hub
* AWS ECR
* Nexus
* JFrog Artifactory

This stage ensures deployment packages are immutable and versioned properly.

Next comes *deployment* to staging environments.

A staging environment behaves similarly to production but is isolated for safe verification. Teams use staging to validate:
* Database migrations
* Service communication
* API compatibility
* Load balancing
* Scaling behavior
* User flows

If staging validation succeeds, deployment proceeds toward production.

Now the CD part becomes interesting.

There are generally two approaches:
*Continuous Delivery* →
deployment is automated until staging, but production release requires manual approval.
*Continuous Deployment* →
even production deployment is fully automated if all checks pass.

Large-scale systems often use advanced deployment strategies such as:
* Blue-Green Deployment
* Canary Releases
* Rolling Updates
* Feature Flags
* Shadow Deployments

These techniques reduce downtime and minimize deployment risk.

For example, in Canary Deployment, only a small percentage of users receive the new version initially. The system monitors:
* Error rates
* CPU usage
* Latency
* Crash reports
* Memory consumption

If metrics remain healthy, traffic gradually shifts toward the new version.
Another major part of CI/CD pipelines is rollback automation.

If deployment causes failures, pipelines can automatically revert systems back to the previous stable version. This is critical because failures in distributed systems are inevitable.

*Monitoring* and *observability* also become deeply integrated into CI/CD architectures.

Modern pipelines continuously track:
* Build success rate
* Deployment frequency
* Failure rate
* Mean recovery time
* Infrastructure health

This transforms deployment from a risky manual operation into a predictable engineering workflow.

One of the biggest advantages of CI/CD is development velocity.

Without pipelines:
* releases become slow
* deployments become stressful
* debugging becomes painful
* teams fear production pushes

With proper CI/CD:
* smaller releases happen frequently
* bugs are detected earlier
* rollback becomes safer
* infrastructure becomes more reliable

Another hidden benefit is standardization.

Instead of every engineer deploying software differently, CI/CD creates one repeatable deployment process for the entire organization.

In distributed systems, consistency in deployment processes is just as important as consistency in databases.

The most important idea behind CI/CD is this:
software delivery itself becomes an automated scalable system.

Modern applications are not only designed to scale for users - their deployment pipelines are also designed to scale for developers.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
At first glance, optimizing reads and optimizing writes may sound like the same problem - making the system fast. But in distributed systems, read-heavy and write-heavy workloads behave completely differently. The architecture decisions for a system serving millions of reads per second are very different from the decisions for a system handling millions of critical writes per second.

The diagram above shows this separation clearly.
In the upper section, the system is optimized for reads. The user request first goes to a cache layer before reaching the database. If the requested data already exists inside the cache, the response is returned instantly without touching the database. Only when a cache miss happens does the request travel to the database. This architecture is common in systems like social media feeds, product catalogs, news websites, trending videos, and profile pages where the same data is read repeatedly by millions of users.

The reason caching works so well is because reads are usually repetitive. Thousands of users may request the same viral post, same video metadata, same trending topic, or same user profile repeatedly. Instead of forcing the database to recompute or fetch the same data every time, the cache stores frequently accessed information inside memory systems like Redis or Memcached. Since memory access is much faster than disk access, response latency decreases drastically.

This optimization becomes extremely important at scale. Imagine Instagram fetching post data directly from the database for every scroll operation. The database would collapse under massive traffic. Caching absorbs most read requests and protects the database from overload.

But reads and writes cannot always be optimized in the same way.

The lower section of the diagram shows a write-heavy architecture. Here, the request directly goes through the API to the database, followed by confirmation. This happens because writes are critical operations. Systems cannot afford to lose or incorrectly process them.

For example, in payment systems, banking applications, stock trading platforms, ticket booking systems, or order placement systems, correctness matters more than speed. If a user sends money, places an order, or books a seat, the system must ensure the write is durable and committed properly before responding.

Caching writes aggressively can create consistency issues.

Imagine updating your password, but due to delayed synchronization the old password still exists in cache. Or imagine buying the last concert ticket while another user sees stale cached inventory. Such problems occur when systems prioritize read performance too aggressively without handling consistency carefully.

This is why write-heavy systems often rely on stronger consistency guarantees. Databases may use transactions, WAL logs (Write Ahead Logs), replication acknowledgments, quorum writes, or synchronous replication before confirming success.

Read optimization focuses on:
* reducing latency
* minimizing database load
* serving repeated requests quickly
* improving scalability

Write optimization focuses on:
* ensuring durability
* preventing data corruption
* maintaining consistency
* handling concurrent updates safely

Another important difference is scalability direction.

Read-heavy systems are usually easier to scale horizontally because cached copies can exist across many nodes globally. CDNs and distributed caches help distribute traffic efficiently.

Write-heavy systems are harder because concurrent writes can create conflicts. Multiple users updating the same resource simultaneously introduces race conditions, locking issues, replication lag, and coordination overhead.

That is why many real-world systems separate read and write paths entirely.

For example:
* Social media timelines are optimized heavily for reads
* Banking systems are optimized heavily for writes
* Messaging systems balance both carefully
* Analytics systems batch writes but optimize reads
* Search engines optimize reads aggressively using indexing

Modern architectures often combine both worlds using CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation). One system handles writes safely while another optimized replica handles reads efficiently.

Why not cache everything including writes?
Because cache is temporary and eventually consistent in many systems. Critical writes must reach durable storage safely before the system acknowledges success.
The deeper lesson in system design is that scalability is not only about handling more traffic. It is about understanding workload patterns. A system serving 1 million reads per second behaves completely differently from a system processing 1 million financial transactions per second.

Good system design starts with one question:
“Is my system read-heavy, write-heavy, or balanced?”

That single answer changes the entire architecture.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Kafka Fundamentals and Event Streaming
content:
At first, many engineers think load testing simply means “sending a lot of requests” to a server. But real-world load testing is much deeper than that. The goal is not just to crash the application - the goal is to understand how the system behaves under stress, where bottlenecks appear, and how the architecture reacts when traffic suddenly increases.

In modern distributed systems, load testing is one of the most important reliability practices because systems rarely fail during normal traffic. Most failures happen during spikes - product launches, sales, cricket match streaming, viral posts, Black Friday events, or sudden traffic bursts from millions of users.

Load testing helps engineers answer critical questions:
Can the system survive 10x traffic?
Which component breaks first?
How much latency increases under pressure?
Does autoscaling work correctly?
Will databases throttle?
Will caches start missing?
Will queues start piling up?

A typical load testing flow starts by identifying realistic traffic patterns. This is very important because fake unrealistic traffic gives useless results. For example, an e-commerce platform may receive mostly read requests during browsing, but during flash sales write traffic suddenly explodes because of payments, inventory updates, and order creation.

That means engineers first define workloads such as:
* Concurrent users
* Requests per second (RPS)
* Read vs write ratio
* File upload sizes
* Peak traffic windows
* Geographic distribution
* Mobile vs web traffic

After defining workloads, engineers simulate traffic using load testing tools like JMeter, k6, Gatling, or Locust. These tools create thousands or millions of virtual users that continuously hit APIs, databases, queues, or web pages.

But the most important part is not generating traffic.
The real value comes from observing the system during stress.

During load testing, teams monitor:
* CPU usage
* Memory consumption
* Disk I/O
* Database query latency
* Cache hit ratio
* Queue lag
* Network bandwidth
* Error rates
* Request timeout percentage
* Autoscaling behaviour

Sometimes the application server is perfectly healthy, but the database connection pool becomes exhausted. Sometimes databases survive, but the cache layer starts missing heavily. Sometimes the infrastructure survives but latency becomes unacceptable for users.

This is why load testing is actually a bottleneck discovery process.

There are multiple types of load testing in system design.
Normal load testing checks expected production traffic.
Stress testing pushes the system beyond limits to find breaking points.
Spike testing suddenly increases traffic rapidly to observe recovery behaviour.
Endurance testing runs traffic for hours or days to identify memory leaks, resource exhaustion, or slow degradation.
Chaos load testing combines heavy traffic with failures like node crashes or network delays to test resilience.

One very important concept during load testing is throughput vs latency.
A system may technically handle 1 million requests per minute, but if response time becomes 15 seconds, the system is practically unusable. Good systems maintain stable latency even during high traffic.

This is why engineers usually define SLOs (Service Level Objectives), such as:
* 95% requests under 200 ms
* Error rate below 1%
* Database CPU below 70%
* Cache hit ratio above 90%

Load testing also validates autoscaling systems.

Many companies assume autoscaling will save them automatically. But during real traffic spikes, new servers may take time to boot, containers may fail health checks, databases may not scale quickly enough, or rate limiters may start rejecting users.

Without load testing, these issues remain hidden until production traffic exposes them publicly.

Caching behaviour is another major area tested during load simulations. Under normal traffic, caches may work perfectly. But during spikes, cache evictions increase, misses rise, and databases suddenly receive enormous pressure. Many real-world outages happen because caches fail indirectly, not because databases initially fail.

Queues and asynchronous systems are also heavily tested. Systems like Kafka, RabbitMQ, or SQS are designed to absorb traffic bursts. But if consumers cannot process messages fast enough, queue lag keeps increasing until the system becomes unstable.

Another important area is database locking and replication behaviour. Under high concurrent writes, lock contention increases, replication lag appears, and consistency delays become visible.

This is why large-scale companies continuously perform load testing before major launches.

Netflix tests streaming infrastructure before major releases.
Amazon tests shopping systems before Prime Day.
Hotstar tests live-streaming systems before IPL matches.
Banks simulate payment spikes before festivals.

Because once production traffic arrives, there is no safe environment left to experiment.

Can a system pass load testing and still fail in production?
Yes - because real-world traffic is unpredictable. User behaviour changes, traffic distribution changes, third-party APIs fail, and network conditions vary globally. Load testing reduces risk, but cannot eliminate uncertainty completely.

That is why modern engineering focuses on continuous testing instead of one-time testing.

The biggest mistake beginners make is thinking load testing is only for huge companies.

Even small systems need it.
A startup with 10,000 users today may suddenly go viral tomorrow. If the architecture has never been tested under stress, even a small traffic spike can cause cascading failures.

The key idea is simple:
load testing is not about proving your system is strong.
It is about discovering where it is weak before users discover it for you.

Happy designing ❤️

title: What actually happens inside a CI/CD pipeline after a develo
content:
What actually happens inside a CI/CD pipeline after a developer pushes code?

The diagram above breaks down the complete software delivery lifecycle into seven major stages:
Code → Build → Integrate → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate

Understanding this flow is important because modern companies do not just automate deployments. They automate confidence.

The journey starts with the Code phase. Developers work on features, bug fixes, and improvements inside source control systems like Git. Agile development primarily lives in this stage because teams continuously write, review, and merge code in small iterations instead of releasing huge changes every few months.

Once code is committed, the Build stage begins automatically. The CI server pulls the latest source code and converts it into a deployable artifact. Depending on the technology stack, this could be a Docker image, Java JAR, React bundle, Python package, or executable binary. The goal here is simple: verify that the application can actually be built successfully.

After building comes the Integrate phase, which is where Continuous Integration truly starts. This is the point where code from multiple developers gets merged together. Individually, every developer's code may work perfectly, but integration often reveals hidden conflicts, dependency issues, version mismatches, or API incompatibilities. CI pipelines help discover these problems immediately instead of weeks later.

Next comes the Test stage. This is usually the longest and most important phase of the pipeline. Automated tests validate that the system behaves correctly before any deployment occurs. These tests may include:

* Unit tests
* Integration tests
* API tests
* UI tests
* Security scans
* Performance checks

The purpose is not to prove the code is perfect. The purpose is to catch as many failures as possible before users see them.

Once tests pass, the application enters the Release phase. At this point the software is considered production-ready. Release management may involve versioning, tagging, artifact storage, compliance checks, approval workflows, or business signoffs depending on organizational requirements.

The next step is Deploy. This is where the software finally moves into production infrastructure. Modern deployment systems rarely replace everything at once. Instead they use strategies such as rolling updates, canary deployments, blue-green deployments, and feature flags to minimize risk.

For example, a canary deployment may expose the new version to only 1% of users initially. If metrics remain healthy, traffic gradually increases until the new version serves everyone.

Many engineers think deployment is the final stage.

It isn't.

The most overlooked part of CI/CD is the Operate phase.

Once software reaches production, the real testing begins. Engineers continuously monitor:

* Error rates
* CPU usage
* Memory consumption
* Request latency
* User behavior
* Infrastructure health
* Business metrics

This is where DevOps extends the CI/CD pipeline. DevOps is not just about deployment automation. It is about continuously operating, monitoring, improving, and maintaining systems after deployment.

Notice something interesting in the diagram.

Agile Development focuses primarily on creating software.
Continuous Integration focuses on validating software.
Continuous Delivery focuses on preparing software.
Continuous Deployment focuses on releasing software.
DevOps focuses on running software.

Each layer extends responsibility further into the software lifecycle.

This evolution happened because modern systems are too complex for isolated teams. Developers can no longer throw code over the wall to operations teams and forget about it. Instead, engineering organizations now own the entire journey from code creation to production reliability.

This shift is why elite engineering teams can deploy hundreds or even thousands of times per day while maintaining high reliability.

The ultimate goal of a CI/CD pipeline is not faster deployment.

The ultimate goal is reducing the time between an idea and a reliable production feature while keeping risk as low as possible.

In modern system design, deployment speed is important.

Deployment confidence is even more important.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
At first glance, choosing between an API gateway like Repose or Kong may look like a simple tooling decision - both sit in front of your backend and handle requests. But in real distributed systems, this choice is not just about routing traffic. It is about how you design the control plane of your entire microservices architecture, and how much flexibility, scalability, and operational control you need at scale.

The diagram above highlights this architectural split clearly.
In the left section, we see a *Repose-style architecture*, which is built around a strict filter-chain model. Every incoming request flows through a fixed sequence of processing steps such as authentication, rate limiting, logging, and transformation. Each filter executes in order, and the request moves linearly through the pipeline before reaching the backend service. This makes the system predictable and easy to reason about.

This model works well in environments where API behavior is relatively stable and policies are centrally defined. For example, in legacy enterprise systems or controlled internal APIs, Repose acts like a disciplined enforcement layer. Every request must pass through the same structured checks, ensuring consistency in security and governance.

However, this rigidity also becomes a limitation at scale. If you want to dynamically add new capabilities, reorder processing logic, or attach different policies per service, the filter-chain model starts to feel restrictive. Every change often requires configuration updates and redeployment, which slows down iteration.

Now contrast this with the right section of the diagram, which represents a *Kong-style architecture*.

Here, instead of a fixed filter chain, the request flows through a *plugin-based execution engine* built on top of a high-performance NGINX core. Each feature such as authentication, rate limiting, logging, transformations, or caching is implemented as an independent plugin. These plugins can be attached, removed, or reordered per route or service, giving far more flexibility in how traffic is processed.

This is why Kong is widely used in modern cloud-native and Kubernetes-based systems. It allows teams to evolve APIs quickly without rewriting gateway logic. One service might require strict authentication and rate limiting, while another might only need logging and caching. Kong supports this dynamic behavior naturally.

The reason this plugin-based model scales better is because it decouples *infrastructure logic from business logic*. Instead of baking everything into a single pipeline, functionality becomes modular. This makes it easier to extend, maintain, and distribute across large microservice ecosystems.

But this flexibility comes with its own complexity. Managing plugins across hundreds of services requires strong governance. Without proper control, configurations can become inconsistent across environments, leading to debugging and observability challenges.

From a performance perspective, Repose follows a simpler sequential execution model, which is easier to predict but less optimized for modern high-throughput systems. Kong, on the other hand, leverages event-driven architecture and NGINX’s efficient request handling, making it suitable for handling large-scale production traffic with low latency.

Another key difference lies in ecosystem maturity. Repose is more aligned with legacy systems and static enterprise deployments. Kong is designed for modern microservices, API marketplaces, and cloud-native deployments where APIs evolve rapidly and require continuous integration with observability tools, authentication providers, and service meshes.

A deeper insight in system design is that this comparison is not just about API gateways. It reflects two fundamentally different philosophies:

Repose represents a *pipeline-centric worldview*, where requests are processed step-by-step in a controlled sequence.

Kong represents a *modular plugin-centric worldview*, where behavior is composed dynamically depending on the system’s needs.

Modern architectures increasingly favor the second approach because distributed systems are no longer static. APIs evolve continuously, services scale independently, and requirements change frequently.

That is why many real-world platforms today adopt Kong-like gateways or similar cloud-native API management layers rather than rigid filter-chain systems.

Ultimately, the deeper lesson is this:

System design is not just about handling traffic. It is about designing how easily your system can evolve.

A rigid gateway enforces discipline. A modular gateway enables agility. The right choice depends on whether your system prioritizes stability or rapid evolution.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Claude is designed as a multi-layered AI system where a user
content:
Claude is designed as a multi-layered AI system where a user request does not directly reach a single transformer model, but instead passes through a carefully orchestrated pipeline of safety, formatting, reasoning, tool usage, and output control stages. This layered design is necessary because modern LLM systems operate in open environments where inputs can be adversarial, ambiguous, or unsafe, and outputs must remain both helpful and policy-compliant.

In the diagram, the system starts with the User Input layer, which is the raw prompt provided by the user. This input is untrusted by default because real-world users may attempt prompt injection, ask unsafe queries, or embed hidden instructions inside otherwise normal-looking text. Because of this, the system never directly forwards the input into the model without processing.

The first major stage is the Safety and Input Filtering Layer. This layer acts as a protective boundary around the model. It checks for malicious instructions, policy violations, toxic content, and prompt injection patterns. It also attempts to sanitize or neutralize parts of the input that could manipulate downstream reasoning. This ensures that the core model receives a controlled and safe version of the request rather than raw user-provided text.

After filtering, the request moves into the Prompt Formatting Layer. This is where the system constructs the final structured prompt that will be given to the model. It combines system instructions, developer constraints, conversation history, and optional tool context into a single coherent format. This step is crucial because LLMs do not just respond to user input alone—they respond to a carefully engineered prompt structure that defines behavior boundaries and context.

Once the prompt is prepared, it enters the Transformer-based Blocks, which form the core reasoning engine of the system. This is the actual LLM, built using stacked self-attention layers and feed-forward neural networks. Inside this block, the model performs token-level reasoning, builds contextual relationships between words, and generates probability distributions over the next tokens. Mechanisms like multi-head attention, residual connections, and layer normalization enable deep contextual understanding and stable training.

From here, the system may branch into two important paths depending on the situation.

One branch is the Tool Interface, which allows the model to interact with external systems. If the model determines that external information or computation is required, it can request tool execution such as web search, code execution, database queries, or API calls. The key idea is that the model does not directly execute these actions; instead, it delegates them to controlled tool systems and then integrates the results back into its reasoning flow.

The second branch is the Safety Refusal Head, which acts as a final decision-making checkpoint for policy enforcement. Even if the transformer generates a response, this component evaluates whether the content is safe to display. If the output violates safety guidelines or contains restricted information, the system can refuse, modify, or block the response entirely. This ensures that unsafe generations do not reach the user.

After these branches are resolved, the response moves into the Response Post-processing Layer. This stage refines the raw output from the model into a clean, structured, and user-friendly format. It may involve formatting improvements, removal of unsafe fragments, consistency checks, and alignment with system-level response policies. This layer ensures the final output is readable, stable, and aligned with expected behavior standards.

Finally, the system produces the Final Output, which is returned to the user as the response.

The key insight from this architecture is that modern LLM systems like Claude are not single-model pipelines. Instead, they are composed systems where multiple specialized layers handle safety, reasoning, tool use, and output control independently.

The deeper design philosophy is separation of concerns:

The filtering layer ensures safety,
the prompt layer ensures structure,
the transformer ensures intelligence,
the tool layer ensures extensibility,
and the post-processing layer ensures controlled delivery.

This layered separation is what allows such systems to operate reliably at scale in real-world environments where both capability and safety are equally important.

In distributed AI systems, intelligence alone is not enough. Control and safety are equally critical.

Happy designing ❤️

title: Redis Caching Fundamentals
content:
When we watch a live cricket match on Hotstar, it feels simple - open the app, click play, and enjoy the game. But behind the scenes, delivering the same ball to millions of viewers at exactly the same time is one of the toughest system design challenges in the world.

Think about an IPL final or a World Cup match. The moment Virat Kohli reaches a century or a batter hits a match-winning six, millions of people open the app simultaneously. If every user request went directly to a central server, the entire system would crash within seconds. That's why streaming platforms rely heavily on CDNs (Content Delivery Networks). Instead of serving video from one location, video segments are distributed across CDN edge servers around the world. Users are connected to the nearest CDN node, reducing latency and preventing backend servers from being overwhelmed.

Another interesting fact is that live video isn't streamed as one huge file. The live feed is broken into thousands of small video segments, each lasting a few seconds. Viewers continuously download Segment 1, Segment 2, Segment 3, and so on. Since millions of users request the same segments, CDN nodes cache them and serve them repeatedly without contacting the origin servers every time. During major sporting events, CDNs often become more important than the application servers themselves.

Behind the CDN sits a load balancer. Its job is to distribute incoming traffic across multiple backend servers so that no single machine gets overloaded. If one server fails, traffic is automatically redirected to healthy servers, ensuring uninterrupted streaming for viewers.

Traffic during live events is highly unpredictable, which is why platforms use Auto Scaling Groups. On a normal day, a few hundred thousand users may be watching. During an IPL final, that number can suddenly jump to tens of millions. Cloud infrastructure continuously monitors CPU usage, memory consumption, network throughput, active connections, and request rates. As traffic increases, new servers are launched automatically. Once the match ends and traffic drops, unnecessary servers are terminated, helping control infrastructure costs.

Bandwidth is another massive challenge. A single HD stream consumes several megabits per second. Multiply that by millions of concurrent viewers, and the bandwidth requirements become enormous. To solve this, streaming platforms use adaptive bitrate streaming. Users with fast internet connections receive higher-quality video, while users on slower networks receive lower-quality streams. The system dynamically adjusts video quality based on network conditions to provide the best possible viewing experience.

One of the toughest challenges is the "thundering herd problem." Imagine a match-winning six. Millions of users may refresh the stream, replay highlights, check scorecards, share clips, or open the app within seconds. These synchronized actions create sudden traffic spikes far larger than normal patterns. To handle such bursts, platforms depend on distributed caching, edge delivery, traffic shaping, queue-based processing, and rate limiting.

Latency is equally important. While sports viewers may tolerate being 20–30 seconds behind the actual event, everyone still expects a smooth and responsive experience. Streaming platforms constantly balance video quality, buffering, latency, and bandwidth consumption. Lower latency often means less buffering, which increases system complexity because there is less room to absorb network fluctuations.

Fault tolerance is another critical requirement. Imagine an entire data center failing during a World Cup final. Millions of viewers should continue watching without even noticing the outage. To achieve this, infrastructure is replicated across multiple availability zones and regions. If one region becomes unavailable, traffic is automatically rerouted to healthy regions.

A common question is: why not simply buy one extremely powerful server? The answer is that computing power is only a small part of the challenge. The real problems are geographic distribution, bandwidth, fault tolerance, latency, and handling millions of simultaneous connections worldwide. No single server, regardless of its power, can solve these distributed system challenges.

The most fascinating part of live streaming architecture is that encoding and storing the video is often the easy part. The real challenge is delivering the same content reliably, at scale, with low latency, to millions of users simultaneously without the entire system collapsing. That's why platforms like Hotstar are not just video applications - they are some of the largest and most sophisticated distributed systems ever built.

Happy designing ❤️

title: When we send a message on WhatsApp, it feels incredibly simp
content:
When we send a message on WhatsApp, it feels incredibly simple. We type a message, press send, and within seconds it appears on the other person's phone. But behind this seemingly simple interaction lies one of the most important security systems used on the internet today: End-to-End Encryption (E2EE).

The goal of end-to-end encryption is surprisingly straightforward. The message should only be readable by the sender and the intended receiver. Not WhatsApp. Not internet providers. Not hackers. Not even the servers that carry the message across the world.

Looking at the diagram, the process starts when the sender wants to send a message. Before any message leaves the sender's device, it is encrypted using a public key associated with the receiver. A public key can be safely shared with anyone and is usually distributed through the messaging platform's servers. The important detail is that while anyone can encrypt data using the public key, nobody can decrypt it using that same key.

Once encrypted, the message travels through WhatsApp's servers. This is where many people have a common misconception. The servers absolutely do handle the message, route it, store it temporarily if the receiver is offline, and ensure delivery. However, the servers only see encrypted ciphertext. They act like a postal service carrying a locked box without possessing the key needed to open it.

When the encrypted message reaches the receiver, the receiver's private key is used to decrypt the content. Unlike the public key, the private key never leaves the receiver's device and is kept secret. Since only the matching private key can decrypt data encrypted with the corresponding public key, only the intended recipient can read the original message.

A common question arises here: if WhatsApp's servers help deliver the message, why can't WhatsApp simply decrypt it?

The answer lies in the cryptographic design. The server only has access to public keys, which are useful for encryption but useless for decryption. The private key required to unlock the message never resides on WhatsApp's infrastructure. Even if the server wanted to read the message, it lacks the necessary cryptographic material.

Another interesting challenge appears when millions of users are continuously exchanging messages. Constantly encrypting every message with asymmetric encryption alone would be computationally expensive. Modern messaging applications therefore use a hybrid approach. Public and private keys are primarily used to establish a secure session, after which much faster symmetric encryption keys are generated for actual message exchange. This provides both strong security and high performance.

What happens if someone intercepts the message while it is traveling across the internet?

Without encryption, the attacker could immediately read the contents. With end-to-end encryption, the attacker only sees random encrypted bytes. Since they do not possess the receiver's private key, the intercepted data is practically useless.

The system also protects against many server-side breaches. Imagine a messaging company's database being compromised. Attackers may gain access to stored encrypted messages, but without the corresponding private keys, those messages remain unreadable. This significantly reduces the impact of infrastructure compromises.

Another important aspect is identity verification. Messaging platforms often allow users to verify security codes or fingerprints. These fingerprints are derived from cryptographic keys and help ensure that users are communicating with the correct person rather than an attacker attempting a man-in-the-middle attack.

A common interview question is: if messages are end-to-end encrypted, how does WhatsApp know who is talking to whom?

The answer is that encryption protects message content, not necessarily metadata. The platform may still know information such as sender IDs, receiver IDs, timestamps, delivery status, and device information. The actual message body, however, remains encrypted and inaccessible to the platform.

End-to-end encryption becomes even more important when messages cross multiple networks, countries, cloud providers, and data centers. Every intermediate system involved in routing the message becomes untrusted by design. Security does not depend on trusting the network. Security depends on cryptography.

The fascinating part is that the internet itself was never designed to be trustworthy. Data passes through routers, ISPs, cloud infrastructure, and countless intermediate systems. End-to-end encryption solves this problem by ensuring that even if every component in the middle can see the message packet, none of them can understand its contents.

The key idea is simple: the server helps deliver the message, but only the receiver possesses the ability to read it.

That separation is what makes end-to-end encryption one of the most powerful security concepts in modern system design.
